# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define the term "Scarifying" as applied to construction equipment. Name the prime mover used and explain its working principle in brief. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

Scarifying is the process of mechanically breaking up or loosening a hard, compacted, or paved surface to a shallow depth of **50 to 300 mm** using tine-like shanks fitted to a motor grader or a rotary soil stabilizer.

The prime mover used is a **motor grader** (or soil stabilizer/recycler for deeper work). The working principle involves lowering a row of **5 to 11 heavy steel shanks** (each tipped with a replaceable tungsten-carbide point) into the ground at a preset depth, and then dragging the shanks forward under the grader's traction. The horizontal force applied through each shank shears and fractures the hard surface. The depth is controlled by hydraulic cylinders, and the spacing of shanks is adjustable to suit the hardness of the material. In a soil stabilizer, a rotating drum with carbide teeth pulverizes the surface through impact and shear instead of pure drag-shear.

---

### Question 2: List any three functional differences between a Wheel Ditcher and a Chain (Ladder) Ditcher. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

| # | Aspect | Wheel Ditcher | Chain (Ladder) Ditcher |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Digging element | A large rotating wheel fitted with cutting teeth on its rim | An endless chain with cutting buckets circulating around a boom |
| 2 | Ditch shape produced | Mostly V-shaped or rectangular, narrow | Mostly rectangular with vertical sides, suitable for utility trenches |
| 3 | Best suited soil | Soft to medium soils, fibrous | Hard, rocky, frozen ground, and deep trenches |
| 4 | Depth range | 0.6–1.5 m typical | Up to 3.0 m or more for utility work |
| 5 | Spoil discharge | Centrifugally flung to one side via the wheel motion | Lifted up and discharged via a cross conveyor |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often write "chain ditcher is faster than wheel ditcher" without specifying soil type — this is **incorrect**. Wheel ditchers are faster in soft soils; chain ditchers are faster in hard rock. Always qualify the answer with the soil condition.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **Internal Choice Note:** As per KTU 2024 ESE pattern, every Module 3 question carries an internal choice between two 14-mark questions. Both Question A and Question B are presented below.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — Functional Details of Mixing and Spreading**

#### Part (a) — Mixing Operation (7 Marks)
With the help of a neat sketch, explain the working principle of a **pugmill mixer** used for soil-cement stabilization. Derive the formula for the **residence time** of material in the mixing chamber. A pugmill has a chamber length of **4.5 m** and paddle axial velocity of **0.08 m/s**. The soil-cement bulk density is **1900 kg/m³** and the effective paddle discharge area is **0.20 m²**. Compute (i) the residence time, (ii) the mass throughput in tonnes/hour, and (iii) the effective output assuming an overall efficiency of **0.78**.

**Model Solution:**

**Working Principle:**
A pugmill is a **twin-shaft, counter-rotating, paddle-type mixer**. The two parallel shafts rotate in opposite directions at **60 to 300 rpm**. Paddles welded to the shafts are angled to push the material axially from the feed end toward the discharge end while simultaneously lifting, folding, and shearing it. The combined effect is a **figure-of-eight flow pattern** that guarantees each particle is exposed to many shear events — producing a homogeneous blend.

**Components:**
1. Feed hopper (aggregate + cement inlet)
2. Twin mixing shafts with replaceable paddles
3. Water spray header for moisture control
4. Discharge chute at the end opposite the feed
5. Gearbox and drive motor
6. PLC control panel

**Residence Time Derivation:**
Let the chamber length be **L** (m) and the axial velocity imparted by paddle angle be **v_axial** (m/s). Since axial velocity is (approximately) uniform across the cross-section for steady-state flow:

$$
t_{res} = \frac{L}{v_{axial}}
$$

**Computation:**

(i) Residence time:

$$
t_{res} = \frac{4.5}{0.08} = 56.25 \text{ s}
$$

This is **> 30 s**, satisfying soil-cement quality criterion.

(ii) Mass throughput:

$$
Q_{vol} = A \times v_{axial} = 0.20 \times 0.08 = 0.016 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}
$$

$$
\dot{m} = 0.016 \times 1900 = 30.4 \text{ kg/s}
$$

$$
\dot{m} = 30.4 \times 3600 / 1000 = 109.4 \text{ tonnes/hr}
$$

(iii) Effective output:

$$
P_{eff} = 109.4 \times 0.78 = 85.3 \text{ tonnes/hr}
$$

#### Part (b) — Spreading Operation (7 Marks)
Explain the **functional details of a mechanical aggregate spreader** used in road construction. Derive the expression relating the **deposited loose thickness** to the conveyor flow rate, spread width, and forward speed. An aggregate spreader has a conveyor discharge rate of **480 kg/min**, opening width **2.5 m**, truck speed **10 m/min**, and loose bulk density **1700 kg/m³**. Find the loose layer thickness deposited. If the compacted thickness required is **75 mm** and the percent swell is **30%**, how many passes are required?

**Model Solution:**

**Functional Details:**
A mechanical aggregate spreader (e.g., the **Blaw-Knox PF-510** or **Caterpillar AP-1055**) consists of:
1. A front-mounted **hopper** of 5–15 m³ capacity to receive material from end-dump trucks.
2. A **variable-speed conveyor belt** at the bottom of the hopper that meters the material out.
3. A **spreading box** with adjustable **vane deflectors** at the rear that spreads the material laterally to a uniform width (typically 2.4–3.6 m).
4. A **prime-mover truck chassis** that carries the entire unit.
5. An **operator control panel** in the cab for adjusting conveyor speed and gate opening.

The conveyor feeds material from the hopper onto the road; the deflector vanes break the falling stream into a uniform sheet.

**Derivation:**
The mass per unit area deposited on the road is the conveyor discharge per unit time divided by the area covered per unit time:

$$
q = \frac{Q_{conveyor}}{w \times v_{truck}}
$$

The loose thickness is then:

$$
t_L = \frac{q}{\rho_{loose}} = \frac{Q_{conveyor}}{w \times v_{truck} \times \rho_{loose}}
$$

**Computation (Loose thickness):**

$$
t_L = \frac{480}{2.5 \times 10 \times 1700} = \frac{480}{42500} = 0.01129 \text{ m} \approx 11.3 \text{ mm}
$$

**Compacted thickness check:**
For one pass, the compacted thickness would be:

$$
t_C = \frac{t_L}{1 + S/100} = \frac{11.3}{1.30} = 8.69 \text{ mm}
$$

**Number of passes for 75 mm compacted:**

$$
N = \frac{75}{8.69} = 8.63 \approx 9 \text{ passes}
$$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Functional Details of Ditching, Bank Sloping, and Finishing**

#### Part (a) — Ditching Operation (7 Marks)
Describe the **working of a wheel-type ditcher** with a neat block diagram. Derive the expression for its **volumetric output in m³/hr**. A wheel ditcher has wheel diameter **2.7 m**, ditch width **0.5 m**, and forward speed **2.5 m/min**. Compute the hourly output in m³/hr. The soil has a swell of **20%**. What is the effective output considering **E = 0.80**?

**Model Solution:**

**Working of Wheel Ditcher:**
A wheel-type ditcher is a **self-propelled crawler or rubber-tired machine** with a large steel wheel mounted on the side. The wheel is fitted with **30 to 60 cutting teeth or buckets** arranged around its periphery. The prime mover (diesel engine) drives the wheel through a gearbox, and the machine also has a separate drive for travel (tracks or wheels).

As the machine advances and the wheel rotates, the teeth bite into the soil, scoop it up, and centrifugal force flings the spoil to one side via the wheel's motion. The ditch is cut in a single continuous pass to the required width and depth. The cutting depth is set by lowering the wheel using hydraulic jacks. A discharge conveyor (optional) can be fitted to load spoil into a truck alongside.

**Components:**
1. Prime mover (diesel engine)
2. Travel drive (tracks or wheels)
3. Digging wheel with replaceable teeth
4. Wheel drive gearbox
5. Hydraulic jacks for depth control
6. Operator station with controls

**Derivation of Output:**
In one revolution of the wheel, the volume of soil excavated is the volume swept by the wheel's periphery across the ditch width:

$$
V_{per\_rev} = A_{cs} \times \pi D_w
$$

where $A_{cs}$ is the ditch cross-section. For a rectangular cut of width **w_cut** and depth **d**, $A_{cs} = w_{cut} \times d$.

The number of wheel revolutions per unit advance depends on the wheel advance per revolution, but for continuous forward motion, the volumetric output per unit time is:

$$
Q = A_{cs} \times v_{travel}
$$

For a V-shaped cut with $A_{cs} = w_{cut} \times d$ (average width interpretation), the formula is often written as:

$$
Q = \frac{\pi D_w^2}{4} \times w_{cut} \times v_{travel} \times 60 \quad \text{[m}^3/\text{hr]}
$$

(The derivation recognizes that the wheel sweeps a circular arc, and integration over one revolution yields this form for typical V-geometry ditches.)

**Computation:**

Given:
- $D_w = 2.7$ m
- $w_{cut} = 0.5$ m
- $v = 2.5$ m/min

$$
Q = \frac{\pi (2.7)^2}{4} \times 0.5 \times 2.5 \times 60
$$

$$
Q = \frac{\pi \times 7.29}{4} \times 0.5 \times 150
$$

$$
Q = 5.725 \times 0.5 \times 150 = 429.4 \text{ m}^3/\text{hr}
$$

**Effective output (with E = 0.80 and swell correction S = 20%):**

$$
Q_{eff} = 429.4 \times 0.80 \times \frac{1}{1.20} = 286.3 \text{ m}^3/\text{hr (BCM)}
$$

#### Part (b) — Bank Sloping and Finishing (7 Marks)
Explain with diagrams the **functional details of bank sloping using a Gradall** and the **finishing operation using a motor grader**. A gradall is required to slope a **5.0 m** high embankment to a slope of **1.5:1 (H:V)**. The bucket capacity is **0.75 m³** (heaped), and the cycle time is **28 s**. With an overall efficiency of **0.82**, calculate the **hourly production** in BCM and the **time required** to complete the slope over a **400 m** length.

**Model Solution:**

**Gradall Functional Details:**
A Gradall is a **telescopic-boom hydraulic excavator** distinguished from conventional excavators by its ability to extend the boom horizontally and vertically while the bucket is at the tip. This makes it ideal for **bank sloping, ditch cleaning, and finishing** because the operator can reach out and down over a slope while the machine sits on level ground above.

Key components:
1. Tracked lower with slewing upper
2. Operator's cab mounted on the front of the upper
3. **Telescopic boom** that extends and retracts hydraulically
4. Stick (or "dipper") with hydraulic cylinder
5. Bucket with cutting teeth
6. Hydraulic pump bank driven by diesel engine
7. Counterweight at the rear

**Working for Bank Sloping:**
The gradall sits on the **berm** (top bench) of the embankment. The boom is extended out over the slope face, the stick is lowered to the desired slope angle, and the bucket is dragged up the slope face while rotating, cutting material and depositing it at the toe. The operator then swings the upper to dump the spoil into a haul truck on the berm or at the toe.

**Motor Grader Finishing:**
A motor grader performs **fine grading** of subgrade, sub-base, or base course to the required cross-section. It uses its **3.7 m long moldboard (middleboard)** mounted on a **rotating circle** between the front and rear axles. The operator adjusts the blade's:
- **Lift** (height above ground)
- **Tilt** (lean left or right)
- **Shift** (offset to side)
- **Angle** (rotation in plan)

In finishing mode, light, overlapping passes at low speed (3–5 km/hr) trim high spots and feather material into low spots, leaving a surface accurate to **±10 mm**. A **stringline or 3D GPS** can be added for automatic control, improving accuracy to **±3–5 mm**.

**Calculation:**

Given:
- Bank height: $h = 5.0$ m
- Slope: $n = 1.5$
- Bucket capacity: $q = 0.75$ m³ (heaped)
- Cycle time: $T_{cycle} = 28$ s
- Efficiency: $E = 0.82$
- Length: $L = 400$ m
- Soil swell (assumed): $S = 25\%$

**Step 1 — Horizontal reach required:**

$$
R = n \times h = 1.5 \times 5.0 = 7.5 \text{ m}
$$

**Step 2 — Hourly production (theoretical):**

$$
P_{theo} = \frac{q \times 3600}{T_{cycle}} = \frac{0.75 \times 3600}{28} = 96.4 \text{ m}^3/\text{hr (LCM)}
$$

**Step 3 — Apply efficiency:**

$$
P_{eff,LCM} = 96.4 \times 0.82 = 79.1 \text{ m}^3/\text{hr (LCM)}
$$

**Step 4 — Convert to BCM (bank cubic metres):**

$$
P_{eff,BCM} = 79.1 \times \frac{1}{1.25} = 63.3 \text{ m}^3/\text{hr (BCM)}
$$

**Step 5 — Cross-section area of slope (triangle):**

$$
A_{cs} = \frac{1}{2} \times R \times h = \frac{1}{2} \times 7.5 \times 5.0 = 18.75 \text{ m}^2
$$

**Step 6 — Total volume in BCU:**

$$
V_{BCU} = A_{cs} \times L = 18.75 \times 400 = 7500 \text{ m}^3
$$

**Step 7 — Total volume in BCM:**

$$
V_{BCM} = 7500 \times 1.25 = 9375 \text{ m}^3
$$

**Step 8 — Time to complete:**

$$
t = \frac{V_{BCM}}{P_{eff,BCM}} = \frac{9375}{63.3} \approx 148.1 \text{ hours}
$$

This corresponds to approximately **18.5 working days at 8 hr/day**.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Mistakes That Cost Marks**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to convert LCM ↔ BCM**: Almost every productivity problem loses a mark if you forget the swell correction $f_{swell} = 1/(1+S/100)$. Always write this step explicitly.
>
> 2. **Mixing units mid-formula**: Students frequently write $\frac{600}{2.4 \times 8}$ without units and then give the answer in "mm" without showing that kg/min divided by (m × m/min) equals kg/m². Always carry units through.
>
> 3. **Confusing "wheel" and "chain" ditcher geometries**: The wheel-arc geometry vs. chain-bucket geometry is distinct. Do not mix them — examiners give zero marks for cross-blending.
>
> 4. **Ignoring efficiency factor**: A productivity formula without an E-factor is "theoretical" and is only a half-answer. Always multiply by **E = 0.75–0.83** for real-world output.
>
> 5. **Using the wrong slope formula**: For a slope of **2:1 (H:V)**, the horizontal run for a vertical height **h** is **2h**, NOT **h/2**. This is the most common slip in bank-sloping problems.
>
> 6. **Drawing the grader blade backwards**: In sketches, students often draw the concave side of the moldboard facing the direction of travel. The **concave side faces the uncut material** (upstream) so that material rolls forward and to the side, not backwards.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

### Rapid-Revision Checklist

- [x] **Stripping** uses bulldozers/scrapers to remove topsoil; standard depth **150–300 mm**.
- [x] **Scarifying** uses motor-grader shanks to break up hard surfaces to **50–300 mm** depth.
- [x] **Mixing** uses pugmills, drum mixers, or travelling mixers; residence time **≥ 30 s** for soil-cement.
- [x] **Spreading** uses motor graders or spreader boxes; output measured in **m²/hr**.
- [x] **Ditching** uses wheel or chain ditchers; standard ditch is **300–600 mm wide, 300–750 mm deep**.
- [x] **Bank sloping** uses gradalls or long-reach excavators; horizontal reach = **n × h** for slope n:1.
- [x] **Snow removal** uses plows and rotary blowers; blower throw velocity from projectile kinematics.
- [x] **Finishing** uses motor graders or trimmer-finishers; tolerance **±10 mm** (grader) or **±3–5 mm** (trimmer with 3D GPS).
- [x] **Universal productivity equation:** $P_{eff} = P_{theo} \times E$, with **E = 0.50–0.83**.
- [x] **Always convert LCM ↔ BCM** using swell factor **S = 1 + swell %**.
- [x] **Carry units through every calculation** — kg/m², m³/hr, tonnes/hr, BCM/hr.
- [x] **Scarifier drawbar pull** = $R_p \times w \times d / \cos\alpha$ per shank.
- [x] **Wheel ditcher output** = $\frac{\pi D_w^2}{4} \times w_{cut} \times v \times 60$ m³/hr.
- [x] **Snow blower power** = $\frac{1}{2} \dot{m} v^2 / \eta$ where η ≈ 0.60.
- [x] **Compacted thickness** = loose thickness / (1 + S/100).

### Equipment Selection Decision Guide

| Task | Best Equipment | Why |
|---|---|---|
| Topsoil removal on a large site | Scraper with bowl lowered | Loads and hauls in one cycle |
| Breaking asphalt crust, 75 mm | Motor grader scarifier, 5 shanks | Lower cost than full recycler |
| Mixing soil-cement in stationary plant | Pugmill | Best homogeneity, controlled residence time |
| In-place soil-cement mixing | Travelling soil stabilizer | No hauling; one-pass operation |
| Ditching in soft soil, 0.5 m deep | Wheel ditcher | High speed, low cost |
| Ditching in hard rock, 1.5 m deep | Chain (ladder) ditcher | Required for rock cutting |
| Sloping 4 m high embankment, 2:1 | Gradall | Safe operation from above |
| Final finish to ±5 mm tolerance | Trimmer-finisher with 3D GPS | Sub-cm accuracy |
| Snow removal on highway | Truck with one-way plow | High speed, low cost |
| Snow removal in city sidewalks | Front-mounted rotary blower on skid-steer | Maneuverable, precise |
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **Scraper (Earthmoving Engineering Definition):**
> A *scraper* is a self-loading (or assisted-loading), hauling, and spreading earthmoving machine that **scrapes, collects, transports, and deposits** a measured layer of loose or semi-loose earth in a single, continuous, integrated operation. It is a hybrid of a **loader**, a **haul truck**, and a **spreader**, combining digging, hauling, and placing functions in one chassis.

The scraper is a **highly versatile unit** of heavy equipment used in bulk earthworks, particularly where the **cut-to-fill distance (haul distance)** is between **100 m and 3000 m** — the range where conventional dozers become uneconomical and haul trucks become inefficient.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight — Classification Criterion:**
> Scrapers are **classified by the source of their tractive power (propulsion)** into two major families:
> 1. **Tractor-Towed (Pull-Type) Scrapers** — rely on an external prime mover (a crawler or wheel tractor).
> 2. **Motorized (Self-Propelled) Scrapers** — have an integral engine that drives the bowl-carrying tractor unit.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Moving Bucket Spoon" 🚜

Imagine a **giant stainless-steel soup ladle mounted on four wheels, pulled by a tractor**, with a hydraulic lid that opens at the top to let soil in and a **rear ejector blade** that pushes the soil out as a neat, even layer. That is a scraper.

- The **bowl** = the ladle
- The **apron** (the front movable wall) = the lid that opens to let earth in
- The **ejector** (rear wall) = the back of the ladle that pushes food out
- The **tractor pulling it** = your arm holding the ladle

When the scraper is loaded, the apron lowers, cutting blades on the bottom of the bowl shave the earth, and the earth piles into the bowl. At the dump site, the ejector slides forward, pushing out the earth in a uniform windrow (a long, narrow heap).

> [!TIP]
> **Why scrapers matter for KTU:**
> In a single machine, they perform what would otherwise require a **loader + 5–10 dump trucks + a dozer** to spread the fill. This compactness is the reason scrapers dominate highway, canal, and airfield construction economics.

### 1.3 Standard Metrics Used in Scraper Engineering

| Metric | Typical Range | Symbol |
|---|---|---|
| Heaped bowl capacity | **8 m³ – 35 m³** | $V$ |
| Rated payload | **15 tonnes – 60 tonnes** | $W$ |
| Haul speed (loaded) | **15–50 km/h** | $v_L$ |
| Return speed (empty) | **30–65 km/h** | $v_R$ |
| Operating cut depth | **150 mm – 500 mm** | $d_c$ |
| Effective cut width | **2.4 m – 3.7 m** | $w$ |

> [!NOTE]
> All values above represent **industry standards** for production-class scrapers and are the figures assumed in KTU numerical problems unless otherwise stated.

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Functional Components of a Scraper

A scraper, irrespective of its propulsion type, is built around **five core sub-systems**:

1. **Bowl (Body / Container):** A large open-top steel box, curved at the front to admit material. This is the cargo container.
2. **Cutting Edges (Blades):** Replaceable steel blades bolted to the bottom of the bowl. They shave the earth as the machine moves forward.
3. **Apron (Front Movable Wall):** A hydraulically controlled vertical wall at the front of the bowl. During loading, it lifts to admit earth. During haul, it closes to retain the load. During spreading, it remains closed while the ejector pushes the load rearward against it.
4. **Ejector (Rear Movable Wall):** A hydraulically powered wall that slides forward inside the bowl, pushing the load out the rear in a controlled layer.
5. **Draft Frame (Hitch Assembly):** A rigid frame that connects the scraper to the towing tractor (in pull-type) or to the rear tractor unit (in tandem motorized scrapers).

### 2.2 The Four-Phase Scraper Operating Cycle

The economics of a scraper revolves around its **cycle time ($T_c$)**, the sum of four phases:

| Phase | Description | Key Variable |
|---|---|---|
| **1. Load (Dig)** | Apron lifts, bowl lowers, tractor pushes scraper, cutting edges shave earth into bowl. | Load time $t_1$ (typically **0.5 – 1.5 min**) |
| **2. Haul (Carry)** | Apron closes, bowl raises, machine travels to fill site at full load. | Haul time $t_2 = L_h / v_L$ |
| **3. Spread / Dump** | Ejector pushes load out as the machine moves forward, spreading a uniform layer. | Spread time $t_3$ (typically **0.5 – 1.0 min**) |
| **4. Return** | Empty machine returns to cut area at higher speed. | Return time $t_4 = L_h / v_R$ |

$$T_c = t_1 + t_2 + t_3 + t_4$$

### 2.3 Classification of Scrapers — Detailed Taxonomy

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 — Module 3 Classification:**

**A. Tractor-Towed (Pull-Type) Scrapers**
- **A.1 Crawler-Towed Scrapers** — pulled by a crawler (track) tractor; excellent for short hauls, soft ground, steep grades.
- **A.2 Wheel-Towed Scrapers** — pulled by a wheel (rubber-tyre) tractor; faster on firm ground, but lower tractive effort.

**B. Motorized (Self-Propelled) Scrapers**
- **B.1 Single-Engine (Conventional) Scraper** — one engine drives the front power unit only; rear wheels are unpowered. Often push-loaded by a crawler dozer.
- **B.2 Twin-Engine (Tandem-Powered) Scraper** — two engines, one on the front tractor unit and one on the rear scraper tractor. Both axles are powered, eliminating the need for a pusher.
- **B.3 Elevating Scraper** — has an internal **elevating loader** (a chain-and-flight conveyor inside the bowl) that mechanically lifts the cut material into the bowl. **Self-loading; no pusher required.**

### 2.4 Tractor-Towed Scrapers — Engineering Description

A pull-type scraper consists of:
- A **scraper bowl** with cutting edges, apron, and ejector.
- A **draft frame (A-frame hitch)** at the front for attachment to a crawler or wheel tractor.
- **No prime mover of its own** — it is dead weight when uncoupled.

**Operating Principle:** The towing tractor provides both **tractive effort** (to move the unit) and **draft force** (to pull the cutting edges through the earth). A **pusher dozer** is typically used behind the scraper to assist loading, since the cutting resistance at the blades can exceed the tractor's drawbar pull.

**Advantages:**
- Lower capital cost (no engine in the scraper).
- Can be unhitched and left at the work site, freeing the tractor.
- Better suited to **rough, muddy, or steep terrain** (when crawler-towed).
- Can be used in tandem — multiple scrapers behind one tractor.

**Limitations:**
- Limited speed (crawler-towed ≤ **8 km/h**; wheel-towed ≤ **25 km/h**).
- Productivity drops sharply beyond **150 m** haul distance.
- Requires a pusher dozer for efficient loading.
- Greater wear on the towing tractor.

> [!NOTE]
> **Typical KTU-specified sizes:** Tractor-towed scrapers have heaped capacities in the range of **8 m³ – 15 m³**.

### 2.5 Motorized Scrapers — Engineering Description

A self-propelled scraper has an **integral power unit** (the front tractor) with its own diesel engine driving the front wheels. The scraper bowl is mounted on its own rear axle (powered or unpowered, depending on type).

**B.1 Single-Engine Scraper:**
- One engine, typically **300 – 500 HP**, on the front tractor.
- Front wheels are powered; rear wheels (on the scraper) are free-rolling.
- **Loading assistance required** from a pusher dozer (a small crawler tractor that pushes the scraper from behind during the load phase).
- Most common in medium-haul earthworks (**300 m – 1500 m**).

**B.2 Twin-Engine (Tandem) Scraper:**
- Two engines, each **250 – 400 HP**, one on the front and one on the rear scraper tractor.
- **All wheels are powered (4×4 or 6×6 drive).**
- Self-loading without a pusher; uses its own tractive effort plus the rear engine's torque to overcome cutting resistance.
- Highest productivity per unit; suited to long hauls (**1000 m – 3000 m**) and adverse ground conditions.

**B.3 Elevating Scraper:**
- A **chain-and-flight conveyor** inside the bowl lifts the cut material upward and rearward into the bowl.
- Self-loading, single-engine, but no pusher needed.
- Ideal for **sandy or loose soils**; poor in rocky or clayey material.
- Faster loading (typically **30 – 45 seconds**).

**Advantages of Motorized Scrapers:**
- High speed (haul and return), hence high productivity.
- Independent operation — no tractor scheduling.
- Tandem units climb **steep grades** (up to **15 %**) under load.

**Limitations:**
- High capital cost (essentially two machines in one).
- Higher fuel and maintenance cost.
- Single-engine units **must wait** for a pusher dozer, causing idle time in poor fleet management.

### 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!TIP]
> The following table is the **complete formula set** you must memorize for KTU 2024 ESE numericals on scrapers. Every variable is defined in standard SI units.

| # | Formula | Meaning | Typical Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $T_c = t_1 + t_2 + t_3 + t_4$ | Total cycle time | min |
| 2 | $t_2 = \dfrac{L_h}{v_L \times 16.67}$ | Haul time (where 16.67 converts km/h → m/min) | min |
| 3 | $t_4 = \dfrac{L_h}{v_R \times 16.67}$ | Return time | min |
| 4 | $Q_b = \dfrac{V \times 3600}{T_c \times S_f}$ | Bank-volume production rate (corrected for swell) | $\text{bank m}^3/\text{hr}$ |
| 5 | $V_b = \dfrac{V}{\left(1 + s\right)}$ | Bank volume from heaped (loose) volume | m³ |
| 6 | $R_r = W \times \dfrac{RR\%}{100}$ | Rolling resistance force | kN or kgf |
| 7 | $R_g = W \times \dfrac{g\%}{100}$ | Grade resistance force (positive uphill) | kN or kgf |
| 8 | $R_T = R_r + R_g$ | Total resistance | kN or kgf |
| 9 | $TE_{req} = \dfrac{R_T}{\eta_t}$ | Required tractive effort (with transmission efficiency) | kN |
| 10 | $P_{acc} = \dfrac{TE \times v}{3600}$ | Power available to overcome resistance | kW |

**Where:**
- $V$ = heaped (loose) bowl capacity [m³]
- $S_f$ = swell factor (1 + swell) — typically **1.25 – 1.40** for common earth
- $s$ = percentage swell (decimal form)
- $W$ = gross loaded weight [tonnes or kg]
- $RR\%$ = rolling resistance percentage — **2 %** (concrete), **5 %** (earth), **10 %** (loose sand)
- $g\%$ = grade percentage (rise/run × 100)
- $L_h$ = one-way haul distance [m]
- $v_L, v_R$ = haul and return speeds [km/h]
- $\eta_t$ = transmission and drivetrain efficiency, typically **0.80 – 0.85**

### 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

Scrapers are the **backbone of linear earthmoving projects**:
- **Highway and expressway embankments** (haul 500 m – 2 km).
- **Airport runway subgrades** (uniform 300 mm lifts).
- **Canal and irrigation channel excavation** (longitudinal cuts).
- **Dam and reservoir construction** (borrowed fill from designated pits).
- **Landfill site preparation** and large **industrial park grading**.

The economic sweet spot is when **cut volume × haul distance** falls in the range **500 m³·m – 5000 m³·m** per hour; outside this range, alternatives dominate.

## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Bank-Volume Production Rate $Q_b$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Starting premise:** The bowl is rated in **heaped (loose) volume**, but the engineer is paid for **bank (in-situ, undisturbed) volume** moved. We must convert between the two using the **swell factor**.

**Step 1 — Relate loose volume to bank volume.**

When earth is excavated, it bulks (swell). The swell factor $S_f$ is defined as:

$$S_f = 1 + s = \dfrac{V_{\text{loose}}}{V_{\text{bank}}}$$

where $s$ is the decimal swell (e.g., 25 % swell gives $S_f = 1.25$).

Solving for bank volume:

$$V_b = \dfrac{V}{S_f}$$

**Step 2 — Express cycles per hour.**

One complete cycle takes $T_c$ minutes. The number of cycles per hour is:

$$N_c = \dfrac{60}{T_c} = \dfrac{3600}{T_c \times 60} = \dfrac{3600}{T_c \times 60}$$

Wait — let me restate correctly:

$$N_c = \dfrac{60 \text{ min/hr}}{T_c \text{ min/cycle}} = \dfrac{60}{T_c} \quad \text{[cycles/hr]}$$

**Step 3 — Multiply cycles per hour by bank volume per cycle.**

$$Q_b = N_c \times V_b = \dfrac{60}{T_c} \times \dfrac{V}{S_f} = \dfrac{60 \times V}{T_c \times S_f} \quad \text{[bank m}^3\text{/hr]}$$

**Step 4 — Match the canonical form.**

The KTU reference form is:

$$\boxed{\,Q_b = \dfrac{V \times 3600}{T_c \times S_f}\,} \quad \text{[bank m}^3\text{/hr]}$$

Both forms are equivalent. The 3600 form uses $T_c$ in **seconds**, while the 60 form uses $T_c$ in **minutes** — keep your units straight.

### 3.2 Worked Example — Numerical from KTU Pattern

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem (14-mark KTU-pattern):** A twin-engine motorized scraper has a **heaped capacity of 24 m³**, a **rated payload of 50 tonnes**, and operates on a haul road with a **rolling resistance of 4 %** and an **uphill grade of 3 %**. The one-way haul distance is **1200 m**, the **haul speed is 30 km/h**, the **return speed is 45 km/h**, and the **load and spread times are 1.0 min and 0.5 min** respectively. The soil has a **swell of 25 %**. Calculate: **(a)** the **bank-volume production rate**; **(b)** the **required total tractive force** during the loaded haul.

---

**Solution — Part (a): Production Rate**

**Step 1 — Compute haul time $t_2$.**

$$t_2 = \dfrac{L_h}{v_L \times 16.67} = \dfrac{1200}{30 \times 16.67}$$

$$t_2 = \dfrac{1200}{500.1} = 2.40 \text{ min}$$

**Step 2 — Compute return time $t_4$.**

$$t_4 = \dfrac{L_h}{v_R \times 16.67} = \dfrac{1200}{45 \times 16.67}$$

$$t_4 = \dfrac{1200}{750.15} = 1.60 \text{ min}$$

**Step 3 — Sum the cycle time.**

$$T_c = t_1 + t_2 + t_3 + t_4 = 1.0 + 2.40 + 0.5 + 1.60$$

$$T_c = 5.50 \text{ min/cycle}$$

**Step 4 — Apply the production formula with swell factor $S_f = 1.25$.**

$$Q_b = \dfrac{V \times 60}{T_c \times S_f} = \dfrac{24 \times 60}{5.50 \times 1.25}$$

$$Q_b = \dfrac{1440}{6.875}$$

$$\boxed{\,Q_b \approx 209.5 \text{ bank m}^3\text{/hr}\,}$$

---

**Solution — Part (b): Required Tractive Force During Loaded Haul**

**Step 1 — Convert payload to kgf-equivalent weight force.**

A mass of 50 tonnes corresponds to a weight force of:

$$W = 50 \times 1000 \times 9.81 = 490\,500 \text{ N} = 490.5 \text{ kN}$$

(In KTU problems, if the answer is to be expressed in kgf, use $W = 50\,000$ kgf directly. We'll use kN here.)

**Step 2 — Compute rolling resistance.**

$$R_r = W \times \dfrac{RR\%}{100} = 490.5 \times \dfrac{4}{100}$$

$$R_r = 19.62 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 3 — Compute grade resistance (uphill is positive).**

$$R_g = W \times \dfrac{g\%}{100} = 490.5 \times \dfrac{3}{100}$$

$$R_g = 14.715 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 4 — Sum total resistance.**

$$R_T = R_r + R_g = 19.62 + 14.715$$

$$\boxed{\,R_T \approx 34.34 \text{ kN}\,}$$

**Step 5 — Apply drivetrain efficiency $\eta_t = 0.85$ (typical) for required tractive effort.**

$$TE_{req} = \dfrac{R_T}{\eta_t} = \dfrac{34.34}{0.85} \approx 40.4 \text{ kN}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This tractive effort is comfortably within the capability of a twin-engine 500–600 HP scraper, confirming the equipment selection is feasible for this haul.

### 3.3 Comparative Analytical Matrix — Tractor-Towed vs Motorized

| Parameter | Tractor-Towed | Single-Engine Motorized | Twin-Engine Motorized | Elevating Scraper |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Haul range (m)** | 50 – 150 | 300 – 1500 | 1000 – 3000 | 200 – 1000 |
| **Speed (loaded, km/h)** | 6 – 25 | 20 – 40 | 25 – 50 | 15 – 30 |
| **Typical heaped cap. (m³)** | 8 – 15 | 15 – 25 | 20 – 35 | 8 – 20 |
| **Pusher dozer needed?** | Yes | Yes | **No** | **No** |
| **Suitable terrain** | Soft / rough | Firm / graded | Firm / adverse | Loose sand / loam |
| **Capital cost** | Low | High | Very high | High |
| **Operator skill** | Low | Moderate | High | Moderate |
| **Fuel consumption** | Low | High | Very high | High |
| **Best for (KTU context)** | Short hauls, dam toes | Highway subgrade | Long canal hauls | Sandy airfield fills |

## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Scraper

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Tractor Power Unit] --> B[Draft Frame Hitch]
    B --> C[Scraper Bowl Assembly]
    C --> D[Cutting Edges]
    C --> E[Apron Cylinder]
    C --> F[Ejector Cylinder]
    C --> G[Hydraulic Reservoir]
    G --> E
    G --> F
    H[Operator Cabin] --> I[Control Valves]
    I --> E
    I --> F
    J[Ground Surface] --> D
    D --> K[Cut Material]
    K --> C
    C --> L[Spread Layer at Fill]
    F --> L
```

### 4.2 Operation Cycle State Machine

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Idle
    Idle --> Loading : Lower bowl, raise apron, push from rear
    Loading --> Hauling : Close apron, raise bowl, accelerate
    Hauling --> Spreading : Reach fill site, lower bowl, advance ejector
    Spreading --> Returning : Empty bowl retracts ejector, apron closed
    Returning --> Loading : Reach cut, lower bowl, open apron
    Loading --> Idle : Cycle complete or operator hold
    Spreading --> Idle : Operator hold
    Hauling --> Idle : Operator hold
```

### 4.3 Scraper Classification Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S[Scrapers] --> TT[Tractor Towed Pull Type]
    S --> MO[Motorized Self Propelled]
    TT --> TT1[Crawler Towed]
    TT --> TT2[Wheel Towed]
    MO --> MO1[Single Engine]
    MO --> MO2[Twin Engine Tandem]
    MO --> MO3[Elevating Scraper]
    MO1 --> MO1A[Needs Pusher Dozer]
    MO2 --> MO2A[Self Loading No Pusher]
    MO3 --> MO3A[Internal Chain Conveyor]
```

### 4.4 Cut-to-Fill Cycle Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph CUT[Cut Area]
        C1[Bowl lowered, apron open]
        C2[Pusher dozer assists]
        C1 --> C2
        C2 --> C3[Bowl full, apron closes]
    end
    subgraph HAUL[Haul Road]
        H1[Raise bowl, accelerate to haul speed]
        H2[Travel loaded to fill site]
        H1 --> H2
    end
    subgraph FILL[Fill Area]
        F1[Lower bowl to spread height]
        F2[Ejector advances, material spread in layer]
        F3[Apron remains closed, machine moves forward]
        F1 --> F2 --> F3
    end
    subgraph RET[Return]
        R1[Ejector retracted, bowl empty]
        R2[Travel empty at high speed back to cut]
        R1 --> R2
    end
    C3 --> H1
    H2 --> F1
    F3 --> R1
    R2 --> C1
```

> [!TIP]
> **Reading the diagram:** The four boxes (CUT, HAUL, FILL, RET) correspond to the four phases $t_1, t_2, t_3, t_4$ in the cycle time formula. This visual is exactly how KTU examiners expect you to annotate a cycle-time numerical.

## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — June 2024]**
*Define a scraper. List any four earthmoving operations it can perform in a single pass.*

**Model Answer:**

A **scraper** is a self-loading (or assisted-loading), hauling, and spreading machine that scrapes, transports, and deposits measured layers of earth in one continuous operation.

The four earthmoving operations performed in a single pass are:

1. **Loading (Digging / Scraping)** — cutting edges shave the earth into the bowl.
2. **Hauling (Transporting)** — the loaded bowl is carried to the fill site.
3. **Dumping (Spreading)** — the ejector pushes the load out as a uniform layer.
4. **Returning (Empty travel)** — the empty machine returns to the cut area.

(3 marks: 1 for definition + 2 for the four operations listed correctly.)

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
*Distinguish between a tractor-towed scraper and a twin-engine motorized scraper in terms of power source, haul distance suitability, and need for a pusher dozer.*

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Tractor-Towed Scraper | Twin-Engine Motorized Scraper |
|---|---|---|
| **Power source** | External — pulled by a crawler or wheel tractor | Internal — two integral engines, one on front unit, one on rear unit |
| **Haul distance** | Best for short hauls (50 m – 150 m) | Best for long hauls (1000 m – 3000 m) |
| **Pusher dozer** | Required for efficient loading | **Not required** — self-loading using its own tractive effort |

(3 marks: 1 mark per correctly addressed aspect.)

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full-Question Choice (14 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU ESE Pattern:** Each Part B question carries 14 marks, split as **(a) 7 marks** and **(b) 7 marks**. You attempt **ONE full question** from the choice of two.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2024]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** *With the help of a neat block diagram, describe the main components and working of a tractor-towed (pull-type) scraper. State its merits and demerits.*

**Model Solution:**

A tractor-towed (pull-type) scraper is an earthmoving machine that has **no prime mover of its own** and is pulled through the cut by a **crawler or wheel tractor**.

**Main Components (with block diagram reference to Section 4.1):**
1. **Bowl** — large open-top steel container that holds the load.
2. **Cutting edges** — replaceable steel blades fixed to the bottom of the bowl, which shave the earth.
3. **Apron** — hydraulically lifted front wall; opens to admit earth, closes to retain it during haul.
4. **Ejector** — rear hydraulically powered wall that pushes the load out during spreading.
5. **Draft frame (A-frame hitch)** — rigid triangular frame at the front for towing connection.
6. **Hydraulic cylinders and pump** — powered by the towing tractor's PTO or an auxiliary engine; actuate the apron and ejector.

**Working:**
- At the cut area, the bowl is lowered, the apron is raised, and the tractor pulls the scraper forward. Cutting edges shave the soil into the bowl. A **pusher dozer** assists from behind to overcome cutting resistance.
- Once the bowl is full, the apron is closed, the bowl is raised, and the tractor hauls the load to the fill site.
- At the fill site, the ejector is activated, pushing the load out as a uniform layer while the machine moves forward.

**Merits:**
- Lower capital cost; the scraper itself has no engine.
- Can be unhitched and left at the work site.
- Better suited to **rough, muddy, or steep terrain** when crawler-towed.
- Multiple scrapers can be towed in tandem by a single large tractor.

**Demerits:**
- Limited speed → low productivity beyond **150 m** haul.
- Requires a pusher dozer for efficient loading.
- Greater wear on the towing tractor.
- Slower cycle time → lower overall production rate.

*(Mark split: components 3 marks, working 2 marks, merits/demerits 2 marks.)*

---

**(b) [7 Marks]** *A tractor-towed scraper has a heaped capacity of 10 m³. The haul distance is 100 m, the haul speed is 12 km/h, and the return speed is 18 km/h. The load time is 0.8 min, the spread time is 0.4 min, and the soil swell is 30 %. Calculate the bank-volume production rate in m³/hr.*

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Compute haul time $t_2$.**

$$t_2 = \dfrac{L_h}{v_L \times 16.67} = \dfrac{100}{12 \times 16.67} = \dfrac{100}{200.04} = 0.50 \text{ min}$$

**Step 2 — Compute return time $t_4$.**

$$t_4 = \dfrac{L_h}{v_R \times 16.67} = \dfrac{100}{18 \times 16.67} = \dfrac{100}{300.06} = 0.33 \text{ min}$$

**Step 3 — Sum the cycle time.**

$$T_c = t_1 + t_2 + t_3 + t_4 = 0.8 + 0.50 + 0.4 + 0.33 = 2.03 \text{ min/cycle}$$

**Step 4 — Apply the production formula.**

Swell factor $S_f = 1 + 0.30 = 1.30$

$$Q_b = \dfrac{V \times 60}{T_c \times S_f} = \dfrac{10 \times 60}{2.03 \times 1.30} = \dfrac{600}{2.639}$$

$$\boxed{\,Q_b \approx 227.4 \text{ bank m}^3\text{/hr}\,}$$

*(Mark split: cycle time 3 marks, swell factor recognition 1 mark, formula application 2 marks, final answer with units 1 mark.)*

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — June 2024]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** *Describe with a sketch the construction and working of an elevating scraper. Mention any two advantages over a conventional single-engine scraper.*

**Model Solution:**

An **elevating scraper** is a self-propelled scraper equipped with an **integral chain-and-flight conveyor** mounted transversely inside the bowl. This conveyor elevates the cut material from the bottom of the bowl upward and rearward, filling the bowl mechanically.

**Construction:**
- **Front power unit** with a single diesel engine (typical 200 – 400 HP) driving the front wheels.
- **Scraper bowl** with conventional cutting edges and apron.
- **Elevating loader** — a chain conveyor fitted with steel flights (scoops) running across the width of the bowl. Powered by a hydraulic motor or a mechanical PTO drive.
- **Ejector** at the rear for controlled spreading.
- **Operator cabin** with controls for the engine, conveyor, apron, and ejector.

**Working:**
- The bowl is lowered to the ground, cutting edges shave the soil, and the elevator flights scoop the soil upward, depositing it at the rear of the bowl.
- Loading proceeds without a pusher dozer — the elevator does the work of lifting material.
- After loading, the apron is closed, the bowl raised, and the machine hauls to the fill site.
- At the fill, the ejector spreads the load as a uniform layer.

**Two advantages over a single-engine conventional scraper:**
1. **Self-loading** — no pusher dozer is required, eliminating one machine from the fleet and reducing idle time.
2. **Faster loading** — typical load time is **30 – 45 seconds** (versus 60 – 90 seconds for conventional), increasing production rate.
3. **Lower labour and fuel cost per bank m³** moved.
4. **Better suited to loose, granular soils** like sand and dry loam.

*(Mark split: construction 2 marks, working 3 marks, two advantages 2 marks.)*

---

**(b) [7 Marks]** *A twin-engine motorized scraper has a heaped capacity of 30 m³ and a gross loaded weight of 65 tonnes. It hauls on a road with a rolling resistance of 5 % and a downhill grade of 2 %. If the haul distance is 1500 m, haul speed 35 km/h, and return speed 50 km/h, with load and spread times of 1.2 min and 0.6 min, calculate: (i) the cycle time, and (ii) the net downhill tractive force available for acceleration, assuming transmission efficiency of 0.85.*

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Compute haul time $t_2$.**

$$t_2 = \dfrac{1500}{35 \times 16.67} = \dfrac{1500}{583.45} = 2.57 \text{ min}$$

**Step 2 — Compute return time $t_4$.**

$$t_4 = \dfrac{1500}{50 \times 16.67} = \dfrac{1500}{833.5} = 1.80 \text{ min}$$

**Step 3 — Compute cycle time $T_c$.**

$$T_c = 1.2 + 2.57 + 0.6 + 1.80 = 6.17 \text{ min/cycle}$$

**Step 4 — Compute rolling resistance (downhill, $RR$ is always positive).**

$$W = 65\,000 \text{ kgf}$$

$$R_r = 65\,000 \times \dfrac{5}{100} = 3250 \text{ kgf}$$

**Step 5 — Compute grade resistance (downhill is negative; it assists motion).**

$$R_g = -W \times \dfrac{g\%}{100} = -65\,000 \times \dfrac{2}{100} = -1300 \text{ kgf}$$

**Step 6 — Compute net resistance.**

$$R_{net} = R_r + R_g = 3250 - 1300 = 1950 \text{ kgf}$$

**Step 7 — Apply drivetrain efficiency to find net downhill tractive force available.**

$$F_{net} = \dfrac{R_{net}}{\eta_t} = \dfrac{1950}{0.85}$$

$$\boxed{\,F_{net} \approx 2294 \text{ kgf} \approx 22.5 \text{ kN}\,}$$

This represents the **surplus tractive force** available to accelerate the loaded scraper downhill, or to overcome any short stretches of adverse grade en route.

*(Mark split: cycle time 3 marks, resistance components 2 marks, net force with efficiency 2 marks.)*

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students lose marks in scraper numericals:**
> 1. **Unit inconsistency in speed–time conversion** — forgetting that km/h must be multiplied by 16.67 to convert to m/min. Always write: $t = L / (v \times 16.67)$.
> 2. **Confusing heaped, loose, and bank volumes** — the heaped bowl capacity is **loose volume**, not bank. You MUST divide by the swell factor to get bank m³ for the production rate. Examiners specifically test this conversion.
> 3. **Sign convention for grade resistance** — downhill is **negative** (assists motion), uphill is **positive** (resists motion). Rolling resistance is **always positive**.
> 4. **Forgetting the pusher dozer requirement** in single-engine conventional scraper descriptive answers — this is a guaranteed 1-mark loss.
> 5. **Listing only "loader + dump truck"** as an alternative to scrapers — examiners expect the answer **"loader + 5–10 haul trucks + dozer for spreading"** to demonstrate understanding of the integrated nature of scraper operation.
> 6. **Mixing up $t_1, t_2, t_3, t_4$** — students often swap spread time and load time. Remember: **L**oad, **H**aul, **S**pread, **R**eturn (L-H-S-R) → $T_c = t_1 + t_2 + t_3 + t_4$.

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **High-density revision checklist for KTU 2024 ESE — Scrapers Module 3:**

- A **scraper** integrates **digging, hauling, and spreading** in one machine — the core concept examiners test.
- **Classification is by propulsion source:** **Tractor-towed (pull-type)** vs **Motorized (self-propelled)**.
- **Tractor-towed scrapers** are cheap, slow, and suited to **short hauls (< 150 m)** on rough ground; they need a pusher dozer.
- **Single-engine motorized scrapers** are fast and productive but need a pusher; suited to **300 – 1500 m** hauls.
- **Twin-engine (tandem) scrapers** have **all-wheel drive**, self-load without a pusher, and dominate **1000 – 3000 m** hauls.
- **Elevating scrapers** have an **internal chain-and-flight conveyor**, self-load, and excel in **loose sandy soils**.
- **Five core components** of any scraper: **Bowl, Cutting Edges, Apron, Ejector, Draft Frame**.
- **Cycle time** is the sum of **load + haul + spread + return** times — every numerical is built on this.
- The **bank-volume production rate** formula is the most-tested: $Q_b = (V \times 60) / (T_c \times S_f)$ with $T_c$ in minutes.
- **Swell factor** $S_f = 1 + s$ converts **heaped (loose) volume to bank volume** — never skip this division.
- **Total resistance** $R_T = R_r + R_g$; remember the **sign convention** for $R_g$ (uphill positive, downhill negative).
- **Tractive effort required** = $R_T / \eta_t$, where $\eta_t \approx 0.80 - 0.85$.
- The **economic sweet spot** for scrapers is cut-fill distance of **100 m – 3000 m**; outside this, dozers or haul trucks win.
- Always label **units** in your final answer; mark loss for missing units is automatic in KTU valuation.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Scraper Loading - Back Track and Chain Track Loading
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEMUT614 - SPECIAL TYPES OF VEHICLES
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Scraper Loading - Back Track and Chain Track Loading

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

A **Scraper** is a self-propelled, single-purpose earthmoving machine designed to perform three sequential operations in a single piece of equipment: **loading** (cutting and gathering earth), **hauling** (transporting material over a distance), and **spreading/dumping** (depositing the material as a uniform layer). The scraper bowl is a steel container fitted with a horizontal **cutting edge** at the base, a hydraulically controlled **apron** at the front, and a hydraulically actuated **ejector** at the rear that pushes the material out of the bowl.

**Loading** is the most energy-intensive phase of the scraper cycle. It refers to the process by which the cutting edge penetrates the soil, shears a thin slice (called the *load slice* or *cut*), and elevates the loosened material into the bowl until the rated capacity is achieved. The two principal loading methods classified by the KTU 2024 syllabus for Module 3 are:

- **Back Track Loading (Push Loading):** A *pusher tractor* (typically a crawler bulldozer or a tandem-arranged push-pull scraper) applies horizontal thrust to the rear bumper of the scraper, while the cutting edge is lowered to slice the ground.
- **Chain Track (Flight) Loading:** A *power-driven chain-elevator assembly* mounted on the floor of the scraper bowl actively conveys the cut soil up and into the bowl as the machine moves forward.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** Module 3 of *Special Types of Vehicles* focuses on the construction, working, and operational kinematics of **non-conventional earthmoving systems**. Self-loading scrapers represent a hybrid vehicle category that combines transport (hauler) and excavating (loader) functions — making them a KTU-favourite topic for descriptive and comparative questions.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are trying to scoop up a pile of sand with a dustpan while standing still — it is nearly impossible. Now imagine someone **pushing you from behind** while you hold the dustpan tilted into the sand pile: the sand fills the pan easily. This is essentially **Back Track Loading** — the pusher tractor is the friend, the cutting edge is the dustpan lip, and the forward momentum forces soil into the bowl.

For **Chain Track Loading**, picture a **ladle being scraped across a sandy beach with a moving conveyor belt running along its bottom**. The belt carries the sand up and into the ladle as it slides forward — no helper needed behind, but the belt only works well with loose, dry sand, not wet clay or boulders.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Industry Standard Terminology (verbatim from KTU Module 3):**
> - *Load Slice Thickness* = typically **150 mm to 300 mm**
> - *Loading Time (Push)* = **0.6 to 1.2 minutes**
> - *Loading Time (Chain Flight)* = **0.5 to 1.0 minutes**
> - *Rated Heaped Capacity* of single-engine scrapers = **8 m³ to 24 m³**
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Working Principle of Back Track Loading (Push Loading)

The scraper is positioned at the borrow pit with the cutting edge lowered to engage the ground surface. A pusher tractor approaches from the rear and makes positive contact with the **rear push-bar** of the scraper. The cycle proceeds in five discrete phases:

1. **Coupling Phase** — The pusher tractor closes the gap (~3–5 m) and couples its push plate with the scraper's rear bumper. Coupling typically takes **5 to 15 seconds** and is the **dead-time** of the cycle.
2. **Loading Phase** — Both machines move forward at a synchronized **loading speed of 2.5 to 6 km/h**. The pusher applies continuous horizontal force (typically **80 kN to 200 kN**) overcoming the cutting resistance of the soil. The cutting edge slices a load of thickness $t_s$ and the soil curls up into the bowl.
3. **Decoupling Phase** — Once the bowl is full (load indicator signals), the pusher tractor applies brakes briefly, decouples, and reverses.
4. **Pusher Return** — The pusher returns to the starting point to assist the next scraper in the fleet.
5. **Apron Lift & Haul** — The scraper raises the cutting edge, closes the apron, and proceeds to the dump site.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why a Pusher is Needed:** A single-engine scraper has insufficient wheel-traction and engine power at the wheels to both propel the loaded machine AND overcome the cutting resistance of the soil. The pusher supplements the tractive force.

**Push-Pull Configuration:** In modern tandem fleets, two scrapers are coupled nose-to-tail. The rear scraper *is* the pusher for the front one. This eliminates the need for a dedicated bulldozer, raising **fleet productivity by 15–25 %**.

### 2.2 Working Principle of Chain Track (Flight) Loading

A scraper equipped with flight loading has a **chain-and-flight elevator** permanently installed in the floor of the bowl. The mechanism consists of:

- Two parallel heavy-duty **roller chains** running along the inside floor and rear wall of the bowl.
- **Steel flights (buckets)** bolted at regular intervals (~300 mm pitch) to the chains.
- A **hydraulic motor** (driven by the scraper's own engine via a power take-off) that drives the upper sprocket of the elevator.

**Operating Sequence:**

1. The cutting edge is lowered to engage the ground.
2. As the scraper moves forward at **1.5 to 4 km/h**, the cutting edge shears the soil.
3. The flights, driven by the hydraulic motor, travel **upwards and rearwards** along the bowl floor, *scooping* the cut soil and depositing it into the bowl.
4. Once the load indicator shows the bowl is full, the cutting edge is raised and the elevator is disengaged.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Limitation of Chain Track Loading:** The flight mechanism works **only in loose, granular, free-flowing soils** such as sandy loam, dry gravel, and pulverized shale. In **cohesive, wet, or rocky soils**, the flights clog, slip, or break. Productivity falls by **40–60 %** in such materials.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Parameter | Formula | Units | Typical Range |
|---|-----------|---------|-------|----------------|
| 1 | Loading Time (Back Track) | $t_L = \dfrac{Q}{q \cdot v_L}$ | min | 0.6 – 1.2 min |
| 2 | Loading Time (Chain Flight) | $t_L = \dfrac{L_{bowl}}{v_L}$ | min | 0.5 – 1.0 min |
| 3 | Pusher Force Required | $F_P = R_c + W_s \cdot \sin\theta + R_r$ | kN | 80 – 200 kN |
| 4 | Cutting Resistance | $R_c = k \cdot b \cdot t_s$ | kN | 30 – 100 kN |
| 5 | Rolling Resistance | $R_r = W \cdot \mu_r$ | kN | 5 – 25 kN |
| 6 | Effective Grade Resistance | $R_g = W \cdot \sin\alpha_{eff}$ | kN | Variable |
| 7 | Production Rate | $P = \dfrac{Q \cdot 60 \cdot \eta}{T_c}$ | m³/hr | 50 – 200 m³/hr |
| 8 | Total Cycle Time | $T_c = t_L + t_H + t_D + t_R$ | min | 4 – 12 min |
| 9 | Number of Pushers Required | $N_P = \dfrac{t_L + t_{couple}}{t_L}$ | — | 1.1 – 1.3 |
| 10 | Flight Conveyor Speed | $v_f = \dfrac{Q}{A_f \cdot \rho_b}$ | m/s | 0.4 – 0.8 m/s |

> **Notation:** $Q$ = bowl capacity (m³), $q$ = volume per metre of travel (m³/m), $v_L$ = loading speed (m/min), $b$ = cutting width (m), $t_s$ = slice thickness (m), $k$ = specific cutting resistance (kN/m²), $W_s$ = weight of scraper (kN), $\mu_r$ = rolling resistance coefficient, $\alpha_{eff}$ = effective grade, $\eta$ = efficiency (0.75–0.85), $L_{bowl}$ = bowl length (m), $A_f$ = flight cross-section (m²), $\rho_b$ = bulk density of soil (t/m³).

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility:** The above formulas are the *backbone* of **fleet planning in construction and mining**. A project manager deciding whether to deploy a push-loaded scraper fleet or a flight-loaded scraper fleet uses these equations to compute hourly production, owning-and-operating cost per cubic metre, and optimum haul-road gradient.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of Pusher Force Equation for Back Track Loading

The pusher tractor must overcome three distinct resistance components during the loading pass. We resolve all forces along the direction of travel.

**Step 1: Identify the resistances.**

During loading, the pusher and the scraper move together as a coupled system. The total resistance is the sum of:

- $R_c$ : Resistance offered by the soil to the cutting edge
- $R_g$ : Grade resistance (component of weight along slope)
- $R_r$ : Rolling resistance of the scraper wheels
- $R_{rp}$ : Rolling resistance of the pusher's own tracks/wheels (small, often lumped with $R_r$)

**Step 2: Express cutting resistance in terms of slice geometry.**

The cutting edge has width $b$ and slices a thickness $t_s$ at a depth $d$ from the surface. For a soil with specific cutting resistance $k$:

$$R_c = k \cdot b \cdot t_s$$

**Step 3: Express grade resistance.**

If the loading path is on a slope of angle $\alpha$ (positive = uphill), the grade component of the scraper weight $W_s$ is:

$$R_g = W_s \cdot \sin\alpha$$

**Step 4: Express rolling resistance.**

$$R_r = W_s \cdot \mu_r$$

where $\mu_r$ is the rolling resistance coefficient (~0.03 for rubber tyres on firm ground).

**Step 5: Apply force equilibrium on the coupled system.**

The pusher must provide a horizontal force $F_P$ equal in magnitude to the sum of all forward resistances:

$$\begin{aligned}
F_P &= R_c + R_g + R_r \\
F_P &= k \cdot b \cdot t_s + W_s \cdot \sin\alpha + W_s \cdot \mu_r \\
F_P &= k \cdot b \cdot t_s + W_s \left(\sin\alpha + \mu_r\right)
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 6: Account for effective grade (hauling penalty).**

The KTU Module 3 convention combines grade and rolling resistance into an **effective grade** $\alpha_{eff}$ where:

$$\sin\alpha_{eff} = \sin\alpha + \mu_r$$

Substituting back:

$$\boxed{F_P = k \cdot b \cdot t_s + W_s \cdot \sin\alpha_{eff}}$$

This is the **canonical pusher force equation** used in all KTU numerical problems.

### 3.2 Numerical Worked Example (KTU-Style)

**Problem:** A single-engine scraper of mass 32 000 kg is push-loaded on a level ground ($\alpha = 0°$). The cutting width is 2.85 m, slice thickness is 0.20 m, specific cutting resistance is 110 kN/m², and rolling resistance coefficient is 0.04. Calculate the pusher force required. Take $g = 9.81$ m/s².

**Solution:**

**Step 1: Compute the weight of the scraper.**

$$W_s = m \cdot g = 32\,000 \times 9.81 = 313\,920 \text{ N} \approx 313.92 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 2: Compute the cutting resistance.**

$$R_c = k \cdot b \cdot t_s = 110 \times 2.85 \times 0.20 = 62.7 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 3: Compute the effective grade (level ground).**

$$\sin\alpha_{eff} = \sin 0 + \mu_r = 0 + 0.04 = 0.04$$

**Step 4: Compute the rolling + grade resistance component.**

$$W_s \cdot \sin\alpha_{eff} = 313.92 \times 0.04 = 12.557 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 5: Sum all components.**

$$\begin{aligned}
F_P &= 62.7 + 12.557 \\
F_P &= 75.257 \text{ kN}
\end{aligned}$$

**Result:** The pusher tractor must deliver at least **75.26 kN** of horizontal force.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Note:** Always convert the scraper mass to weight using $W = mg$ before applying the formula. Many students forget the gravitational conversion and lose 2 marks in numerical questions.

### 3.3 Derivation of Flight Conveyor Speed for Chain Track Loading

The flights must transport a volumetric flow rate equal to the rate at which the cutting edge slices material.

**Step 1: Volume of material sliced per unit forward distance.**

For slice thickness $t_s$ and width $b$:

$$q = b \cdot t_s \quad [\text{m}^3/\text{m}]$$

**Step 2: Volumetric inflow rate to the bowl.**

If the scraper advances at $v_L$ (m/min):

$$Q_{in} = b \cdot t_s \cdot v_L \quad [\text{m}^3/\text{min}]$$

**Step 3: Conveying capacity of the flight system.**

Each flight has a cross-sectional carrying area $A_f$ (m²) and the chain moves at linear speed $v_f$ (m/s). The carrying rate is:

$$Q_{flight} = A_f \cdot v_f$$

**Step 4: Steady-state condition.**

For a full bowl, inflow must equal outflow (no accumulation):

$$\begin{aligned}
A_f \cdot v_f &= b \cdot t_s \cdot v_L \\
v_f &= \dfrac{b \cdot t_s \cdot v_L}{A_f}
\end{aligned}$$

This is the **minimum flight speed** required to prevent bowl spillage. In practice, designers use a 1.2–1.4× safety factor.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Back Track Loading Cycle

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Scraper at Borrow Pit] --> B[Cutting Edge Lowered]
    B --> C[Pusher Tractor Approaches]
    C --> D[Coupling of Push Bar]
    D --> E[Synchronized Forward Motion]
    E --> F[Cutting Edge Slices Soil]
    F --> G[Soil Curls into Bowl]
    G --> H{Load Indicator ON?}
    H -- No --> E
    H -- Yes --> I[Pusher Decouples]
    I --> J[Pusher Returns to Queue]
    J --> K[Scraper Raises Edge]
    K --> L[Scraper Proceeds to Dump]
    L --> M[Cycle Complete]
    M --> A
```

### 4.2 Block Diagram of Chain-Flight Loading Mechanism

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    ENG[Scraper Engine] --> PTO[Power Take-Off]
    PTO --> HM[Hydraulic Motor]
    HM --> SP1[Upper Drive Sprocket]
    SP1 --> CHAIN[Roller Chain with Flights]
    CHAIN --> SP2[Lower Idler Sprocket]
    CHAIN --> BOWL[Bowl Floor]
    CUT[Cutting Edge] --> BOWL
    BOWL --> SOIL[Soil Elevation Path]
    SOIL --> APRON[Apron Closes on Full Load]
```

### 4.3 Comparative Functional Block — Push vs Flight Loading

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q[Loading Method Selection] --> R{Soil Type?}
    R -- Loose Granular --> S[Flight Loading Viable]
    R -- Stiff Cohesive / Rock --> T[Push Loading Required]
    R -- Mixed --> U[Push-Push with Two Scrapers]
    S --> S1[Self-Contained, No Helper]
    T --> T1[Requires Pusher Tractor]
    U --> U1[Push-Pull Tandem Configuration]
    S1 --> X[Productivity 80-150 m3/hr]
    T1 --> Y[Productivity 100-200 m3/hr]
    U1 --> Z[Productivity 120-220 m3/hr]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagrams:** Each rectangle represents a *functional state* of the system; each arrow represents a *transition event*. In the KTU 2024 valuation scheme, a labelled block diagram carries **2 to 3 marks** by itself in descriptive questions.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper]**
*Define a scraper. List any three earthmoving operations performed by it.*

**Model Answer (verbatim KTU style):**
A scraper is a self-propelled, single-purpose earthmoving machine that loads, hauls, and dumps (or spreads) earth in continuous cycles. Its three primary operations are:
1. **Loading** — the cutting edge slices soil and the bowl fills.
2. **Hauling** — the loaded scraper travels to the disposal site.
3. **Dumping/Spreading** — the ejector pushes the material out as a uniform layer.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Supplementary]**
*Mention any three advantages of chain-track (flight) loading over back-track loading.*

**Model Answer:**
1. **No pusher tractor is required** — the scraper is fully self-sufficient, reducing fleet capital cost.
2. **Single-operator machine** — no coordination between two drivers is needed, reducing cycle variability.
3. **Better fuel efficiency per m³** in loose soils because there is no second engine idling between push cycles.
4. (Bonus) Continuous loading without decoupling dead-time.

---

### Part B — 14-Mark Questions (ESE Pattern, Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing "self-propelled" with "self-loading"** — single-engine scrapers need a pusher; they are self-propelled but not self-loading.
> 2. **Forgetting to convert tonnes to kN** when applying $W = mg$ in numericals — deducts 1 mark.
> 3. **Writing "chain track" instead of "chain-and-flight elevator"** — examiners expect the full term in 7-mark descriptive sub-parts.
> 4. **Skipping a free-body diagram** in force-balance problems — KTU 2024 awards 1 mark for a clean labelled diagram.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — ESE Choice Option 1**

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2024, Expected Pattern]**

*(a)* Explain with a neat block diagram the working principle of **Back Track (Push) Loading** in a single-engine scraper. Mention the role of the pusher tractor and the sequence of operations during a typical loading cycle. **(7 marks)**

*(b)* A single-engine scraper has the following specifications:
- Mass (empty): 28 000 kg
- Cutting width: 2.74 m
- Slice thickness: 0.18 m
- Specific cutting resistance: 95 kN/m²
- Rolling resistance coefficient: 0.035
- Loading path gradient: 3 % (uphill)
- Loading speed: 4 km/h
- Rated heaped capacity: 18 m³

Calculate:
1. The pusher force required at the start of the loading pass.
2. The volumetric loading rate.
3. The theoretical loading time. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution — Part (a):**

**1. Definition:** Back track loading (also called push loading) is a method in which an auxiliary tractor — called a pusher — applies a horizontal pushing force to the rear of the scraper to assist the cutting edge in slicing and gathering the soil into the bowl.

**2. Role of the Pusher Tractor:** The pusher tractor (typically a crawler bulldozer, rubber-tyred dozer, or a second scraper in push-pull configuration) supplements the tractive effort of the scraper's drive wheels. Without it, the wheel-slip at the drive tyres would be excessive and the cutting edge could not penetrate stiff soils.

**3. Sequence of Operations (with reference to the block diagram in Section 4.1):**
- **Positioning:** Scraper is aligned at the cut face with the cutting edge raised.
- **Edge Lowering:** Hydraulic cylinder lowers the cutting edge until it bites the soil.
- **Coupling:** Pusher approaches and makes firm contact with the scraper's push bar (~5–15 s).
- **Synchronized Travel:** Both machines move forward at 2.5–6 km/h; soil is sheared and curled into the bowl.
- **Load Detection:** Load-sensing mechanism (or operator judgement) detects full bowl.
- **Decoupling & Return:** Pusher brakes, decouples, and reverses to assist the next scraper.
- **Haul Preparation:** Scraper raises the cutting edge, closes the apron, and proceeds to the dump site.

**4. Advantages:** High productivity in cohesive soils, larger bowl loading possible, suitable for tough ground.
**5. Limitations:** Requires a second machine and operator; coordination dead-time; not economical for very small jobs.

**Model Solution — Part (b):**

**Step 1: Compute weight of the empty scraper.**

$$W_s = 28\,000 \times 9.81 = 274\,680 \text{ N} \approx 274.68 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 2: Compute the grade angle for 3 % gradient.**

A 3 % gradient means rise of 3 m per 100 m, so:

$$\sin\alpha = 0.03 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \alpha \approx 1.72°$$

**Step 3: Compute effective grade.**

$$\sin\alpha_{eff} = \sin\alpha + \mu_r = 0.03 + 0.035 = 0.065$$

**Step 4: Compute cutting resistance.**

$$R_c = k \cdot b \cdot t_s = 95 \times 2.74 \times 0.18 = 46.844 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 5: Compute rolling + grade resistance.**

$$W_s \cdot \sin\alpha_{eff} = 274.68 \times 0.065 = 17.854 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 6: Total pusher force.**

$$F_P = 46.844 + 17.854 = 64.698 \text{ kN} \approx \mathbf{64.7 \text{ kN}}$$

**Step 7: Volumetric loading rate.**

Volume sliced per metre of travel:

$$q = b \cdot t_s = 2.74 \times 0.18 = 0.4932 \text{ m}^3/\text{m}$$

Loading speed: $v_L = 4 \text{ km/h} = 4000/60 = 66.67 \text{ m/min}$

$$Q_L = q \cdot v_L = 0.4932 \times 66.67 = 32.88 \text{ m}^3/\text{min}$$

**Step 8: Theoretical loading time.**

$$t_L = \dfrac{Q}{Q_L} = \dfrac{18}{32.88} = 0.547 \text{ min} \approx \mathbf{32.85 \text{ seconds}}$$

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — ESE Choice Option 2**

**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper Pattern, KTU 2024 Scheme]**

*(a)* With a neat schematic, describe the **Chain Track (Flight) Loading** system in a self-loading scraper. Discuss its components, working, and the conditions under which it is preferred over push loading. **(7 marks)**

*(b)* A flight-loaded scraper advances at 3 km/h and slices a slice of 0.22 m thickness × 2.90 m width. The bulk density of the in-situ soil is 1.85 t/m³ and the swell factor is 1.25. If each flight has a carrying cross-section of 0.04 m², calculate:
1. The volumetric inflow rate (in-place and loose).
2. The minimum flight conveyor speed required to prevent spillage. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution — Part (a):**

**1. Definition:** Chain Track (also called flight or elevator) loading is a self-loading method in which a powered chain-and-flight conveyor built into the floor of the scraper bowl actively elevates the cut soil up into the bowl as the machine moves forward. It is used in **self-loading scrapers** that require no pusher.

**2. Main Components:**
- **Cutting edge** — at the bottom front, slices the soil.
- **Chain-elevator** — two parallel roller chains fitted with steel flights, running the full length of the bowl floor and up the rear wall.
- **Drive sprocket (top)** — powered by a hydraulic motor linked to the engine's PTO.
- **Idler sprocket (bottom)** — tensioned and free-wheeling.
- **Hydraulic motor & gear train** — drives the upper sprocket.
- **Apron** — front gate that prevents spillage during loading.
- **Load indicator** — signals when the bowl is full.

**3. Working Principle:** As the scraper advances, the cutting edge shears a thin slice. The flights, driven by the hydraulic motor, travel *upward and rearward* along the bowl floor at linear speed $v_f$. They engage the cut soil, scoop it up, and carry it to the top of the bowl. The load is complete when the apron is fully packed.

**4. Conditions of Preference over Push Loading:**
- Soil is **loose, granular, free-flowing** (sandy loam, dry gravel, pulverized shale).
- Job site is **small to medium** where deploying a pusher is uneconomical.
- **Single-operator fleets** are preferred.
- Haul distances are **short to medium** (≤ 1 km) where cycle time matters more than pusher coordination.

**5. Limitations:**
- Ineffective in cohesive, wet, or rocky soils.
- Higher initial machine cost.
- Chain and flight maintenance is expensive.

**Model Solution — Part (b):**

**Step 1: Volume sliced per metre (in-place).**

$$q_{ip} = b \cdot t_s = 2.90 \times 0.22 = 0.638 \text{ m}^3/\text{m}$$

**Step 2: Convert to loose volume using swell factor.**

$$q_{loose} = q_{ip} \cdot S = 0.638 \times 1.25 = 0.7975 \text{ m}^3/\text{m}$$

**Step 3: Convert speed to m/min.**

$$v_L = 3 \text{ km/h} = 50 \text{ m/min}$$

**Step 4: Volumetric inflow rate (in-place).**

$$Q_{ip} = q_{ip} \cdot v_L = 0.638 \times 50 = 31.9 \text{ m}^3/\text{min}$$

**Step 5: Volumetric inflow rate (loose).**

$$Q_{loose} = q_{loose} \cdot v_L = 0.7975 \times 50 = 39.875 \text{ m}^3/\text{min}$$

**Step 6: Minimum flight speed (in m/s).**

Convert $Q_{loose}$ to m³/s: $39.875 / 60 = 0.6646 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$.

$$v_f = \dfrac{Q_{loose}}{A_f} = \dfrac{0.6646}{0.04} = 16.615 \text{ m/s}$$

This is unrealistically high, indicating a design mismatch. In practice, a scraper with these dimensions would not be flight-loaded — the result shows the engineer must increase $A_f$ (more flights) or reduce slice thickness.

**Practical Design Conclusion:** Multiply the theoretical value by 0.6 (packing efficiency):

$$v_{f,\text{design}} = 16.615 \times 0.6 \approx 9.97 \text{ m/s (theoretical floor)}$$

In real designs, $A_f$ is increased so $v_f$ falls in the **0.4–0.8 m/s** range.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Scraper** = self-propelled machine for **load + haul + dump** in one unit.
- **Back Track Loading** uses a **pusher tractor** to apply horizontal force ($F_P = k \cdot b \cdot t_s + W_s \cdot \sin\alpha_{eff}$).
- **Chain Track Loading** uses a **powered flight elevator** in the bowl floor — fully self-loading but works only in **loose granular soils**.
- **Loading speed** range: **1.5 to 6 km/h** (slower for stiffer soils).
- **Loading time** typical: **30 to 75 seconds** for both methods.
- **Pusher force** typical: **80 to 200 kN**.
- **Push-Pull** tandem configuration eliminates dedicated pusher — improves fleet productivity by **15–25 %**.
- **Flight conveyor speed** is governed by inflow rate: $v_f = (b \cdot t_s \cdot v_L) / A_f$.
- **Swell factor** must be applied when converting in-place volume to loose volume in chain-flight calculations.
- **Effective grade** = actual grade + rolling resistance coefficient — a KTU favourite concept.
- Always **convert mass to weight** ($W = mg$) before plugging into force equations.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->
