### **Question B (14 Marks): `[KTU University Exam – May 2023, OEMUT614]`**

**Q: (a)** With the aid of a **free-body diagram and kinematic sketch**, derive the expression for the **mechanical advantage and lift height** of a single-stage scissor linkage mechanism. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A scissors lift truck has the following specifications: **arm length L = 1.5 m, load mass m = 2500 kg, platform length Lp = 1.2 m, system pressure P = 180 bar, safety factor SF = 2.5.** Calculate **(i)** the maximum lift height, **(ii)** the minimum mechanical advantage at maximum lift, **(iii)** the required cylinder bore diameter, and **(iv)** the required cylinder stroke to achieve maximum lift. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for (a) — 7 Marks

**Free-Body Diagram Description (1 mark):**
The scissor linkage consists of two crossed arms of equal length $L$, pinned at three points:
- **Ground pivot (A)** — fixed to chassis
- **Central pivot (B)** — where cylinder force is applied
- **Top pivot (C)** — where load/platform is attached

A horizontal force $F_c$ acts at B (from the hydraulic cylinder), and a vertical load $W$ acts at C (from the platform and payload).

**Derivation of Lift Height (3 marks):**

Let $\theta$ be the angle each arm makes with the horizontal.

Vertical height of platform above ground pivot:
$$h = L \sin(\theta)$$

Horizontal displacement of the central pivot (B) from its initial position when arms are flat ($\theta = 0°$):
$$x_B = \frac{L}{2} \cos(\theta)$$

Initially: $x_{B,0} = L/2$.

After cylinder extends by stroke $s$, the midpoint moves inward:
$$\frac{L}{2} \cos(\theta) = \frac{L}{2} - s$$

Solving for $\cos(\theta)$:
$$\cos(\theta) = 1 - \frac{2s}{L}$$

Lift height:
$$h = L \sin(\theta) = L\sqrt{1 - \cos^2(\theta)}$$

$$h = L\sqrt{1 - \left(1 - \frac{2s}{L}\right)^2}$$

$$\boxed{h = 2\sqrt{s(L - s)}}$$

Maximum lift height occurs at $s = L/2$:
$$h_{max} = 2\sqrt{\frac{L}{2} \cdot \frac{L}{2}} = L$$

**Derivation of Mechanical Advantage (3 marks):**

Taking moments about the ground pivot (A):

Applied moment (by cylinder):
$$M_{applied} = F_c \cdot \frac{L}{2} \cdot \cos(\theta)$$

Resisting moment (by load at horizontal distance $x_C = L\cos(\theta) + L_p/2$ from A):
$$M_{resisting} = W \cdot \left[L\cos(\theta) + \frac{L_p}{2}\right]$$

Equilibrium:
$$F_c \cdot \frac{L}{2} \cdot \cos(\theta) = W \cdot \left[L\cos(\theta) + \frac{L_p}{2}\right]$$

Mechanical advantage:
$$\boxed{MA = \frac{W}{F_c} = \frac{L \cos(\theta)}{2[L \cos(\theta) + L_p/2]}}$$

At $\theta = 0$ (arms flat): $MA = \dfrac{L}{2L + L_p}$ (minimum)

At $\theta = 90°$ (arms vertical): $MA = \dfrac{1}{2}$ (maximum theoretical)

---

#### Model Solution for (b) — 7 Marks

**Given:**
- $L = 1.5$ m, $m = 2500$ kg, $L_p = 1.2$ m, $P = 180$ bar $= 18 \times 10^6$ Pa, $SF = 2.5$, $g = 9.81$ m/s²

**(i) Maximum Lift Height (1 mark):**

For a single-stage scissor, $h_{max} = L$:
$$h_{max} = 1.5 \text{ m}$$

**(ii) Minimum Mechanical Advantage at Maximum Lift (2 marks):**

At maximum lift, $\theta = 90°$ in theory, but realistically the arms cannot reach vertical (geometric interference with cylinder). Assuming $\theta_{max} = 80°$ for practical design:

$$\cos(80°) = 0.174$$

$$MA_{min} = \frac{1.5 \cdot 0.174}{2[1.5 \cdot 0.174 + 0.6]} = \frac{0.261}{2 \cdot 0.861} = \frac{0.261}{1.722} = 0.151$$

Alternatively, using $\theta = 90°$ (ideal maximum):
$$MA_{min,ideal} = \frac{1}{2} = 0.5$$

**(iii) Required Cylinder Bore Diameter (2.5 marks):**

Load weight:
$$W = m \cdot g = 2500 \cdot 9.81 = 24525 \text{ N}$$

Required lift force with safety factor:
$$F_{lift} = SF \cdot W = 2.5 \cdot 24525 = 61312.5 \text{ N}$$

Required cylinder force (using $MA_{min} = 0.151$):
$$F_c = \frac{F_{lift}}{MA_{min}} = \frac{61312.5}{0.151} = 406043 \text{ N}$$

Required piston area:
$$A_p = \frac{F_c}{P} = \frac{406043}{18 \times 10^6} = 2.256 \times 10^{-2} \text{ m}^2 = 225.6 \text{ cm}^2$$

Bore diameter:
$$D = \sqrt{\frac{4 A_p}{\pi}} = \sqrt{\frac{4 \cdot 2.256 \times 10^{-2}}{\pi}} = \sqrt{2.872 \times 10^{-2}} = 0.1695 \text{ m}$$

$$\boxed{D \approx 170 \text{ mm}}$$

**(iv) Required Cylinder Stroke (1.5 marks):**

Maximum lift occurs at $s = L/2$:
$$s_{max} = \frac{L}{2} = \frac{1.5}{2} = 0.75 \text{ m} = 750 \text{ mm}$$

Verification: At $s = 0.75$ m:
$$h = 2\sqrt{0.75 \cdot (1.5 - 0.75)} = 2\sqrt{0.75 \cdot 0.75} = 2 \cdot 0.75 = 1.5 \text{ m} = L \checkmark$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Do not confuse MA direction:** The MA of a scissor linkage is **less than 1** for typical platform loads — the cylinder must apply a force **larger** than the load. Students often invert this and lose 2–3 marks.
> 2. **Always include the platform length term** in the MA derivation. Forgetting $L_p/2$ results in the simplified MA = 0.5, which is valid only for point loading at the top pivot.
> 3. **Unit conversion errors:** 1 bar = 10⁵ Pa. Many students write $P = 180$ Pa, which is a fatal error.
> 4. **Safety factor must be applied** to the load, not the cylinder force. Examiners specifically check this.
> 5. **Sketch quality matters:** KTU examiners award 1–2 marks for a clean, labeled free-body diagram. Use proper arrows for forces and clearly mark the pivot points.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Scissors Lift Trucks**

**Core Concept:**
- [x] Scissors lift truck = **hydraulic + scissor linkage + platform/fork** material handling vehicle.
- [x] Uses **pantograph mechanism** for pure vertical lift.
- [x] Single-degree-of-freedom planar linkage.

**Key Formulas (must memorize):**
- [x] Lift height: $h = 2\sqrt{s(L-s)}$, with $h_{max} = L$
- [x] Mechanical advantage: $MA = \dfrac{L \cos(\theta)}{2[L \cos(\theta) + L_p/2]}$
- [x] Cylinder force: $F_c = \dfrac{SF \cdot m \cdot g}{MA}$
- [x] Cylinder bore: $D = \sqrt{\dfrac{4 F_c}{\pi P}}$
- [x] Stroke: $s_{max} = L/2$

**Standard Values:**
- [x] System pressure: **160–250 bar**
- [x] Load capacity: **1–4 tons**
- [x] Lift height: **0.8–6 m**
- [x] Battery: **24V / 48V DC**
- [x] Safety factor: **≥ 2.0**
- [x] Gravitational acceleration: **9.81 m/s²**

**Applications:**
- [x] Warehouse pallet stacking (narrow aisles)
- [x] Manufacturing line-side feeding
- [x] Aviation ground support (catering, baggage)
- [x] Retail back-of-store replenishment
- [x] Automotive service workshops

**Advantages:**
- [x] Compact footprint (narrow aisle operation)
- [x] Pure vertical lift (no load slippage)
- [x] Low cost and low maintenance
- [x] Zero emissions (electric variants)
- [x] Easy operator training

**Disadvantages:**
- [x] Limited lift height (vs forklifts)
- [x] Slower lift speed
- [x] Mechanical advantage < 1 (high cylinder force needed)
- [x] Not suitable for very heavy outdoor loads
- [x] Multi-stage variants are expensive

**Safety Standards:**
- [x] IS 11057:1984 (Indian Standard)
- [x] ANSI/ITSDF B56.1 (US Standard)
- [x] OSHA 1910.178 (US Regulation)
- [x] Tilt sensor cutoff at **3°**
- [x] Mandatory deadman switch and emergency reverse

**Exam Strategy:**
- [x] Always draw a labeled FBD for any MA derivation question.
- [x] State all assumptions (frictionless pins, quasi-static, point/distributed load).
- [x] Carry out units in SI throughout the calculation.
- [x] Apply safety factor to load weight, not to cylinder force.
- [x] Mention relevant IS/ANSI standards for full-mark answers.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Compaction Vehicles: General Description, Specification and Functions
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEMUT614 - SPECIAL TYPES OF VEHICLES
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Compaction Vehicles: General Description, Specification and Functions

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

A **Compaction Vehicle** (also called a **Compactor** or **Compaction Equipment**) is a specialized heavy-duty off-highway construction machine designed and engineered to apply controlled, repetitive mechanical energy (static weight, impact, vibration, or kneading action) to soil, aggregates, asphalt, or waste materials in order to **increase their density**, **reduce air voids**, **enhance shear strength**, **improve bearing capacity**, and **minimize permeability** through particle rearrangement.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** Compaction vehicles are earthmoving/construction machines that densify soil or pavement layers by means of static load, vibratory action, or impact force, thereby achieving specified field density requirements for engineering structures such as highways, railways, earth dams, and airfield pavements.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

Imagine you are packing clothes into a suitcase for travel.

- If you simply place them loosely, the suitcase looks full but zips with difficulty. **Lifting and dropping** the suitcase packs them tighter (this is **impact/ramming compaction**).
- If you sit on the suitcase and roll back and forth, your body weight squeezes the clothes uniformly (this is **static weight compaction**).
- If you shake the suitcase violently while pressing down, the clothes settle dramatically into the available space (this is **vibratory compaction**).

The **"suitcase"** is the **soil layer**, **"clothes"** are the **soil particles**, and **"you"** are the **compactor's drum or plate**. The goal in every method is the same — **get the most material into the smallest possible space**, just like a civil engineer wants the densest possible soil or asphalt layer for a stable road.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Compaction Matters in Engineering:**
> - A well-compacted subgrade can support traffic loads **5× longer** than loose soil.
> - Properly compacted soil has **lower settlement**, **higher CBR (California Bearing Ratio)**, and **minimal water seepage**.

---

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

The following engineering constants and metrics govern compaction performance:

| Metric | Symbol | Standard Value/Unit |
|---|---|---|
| Acceleration due to gravity | $g$ | **9.81 m/s²** |
| Dynamic Centrifugal Force | $F_c$ | **kN to 400+ kN** |
| Vibration Frequency | $f$ | **25 Hz – 80 Hz** (typically 30–40 Hz) |
| Nominal Amplitude | $A$ | **0.4 mm – 2.0 mm** |
| Static Linear Load | $q_l$ | **15–75 kg/cm** |
| Drum Diameter | $D$ | **0.9 m – 2.0 m** |
| Drum Width | $W$ | **1.2 m – 2.5 m** |
| Operating Mass | $M$ | **1 ton – 20 ton** |
| Travel Speed | $v$ | **1 km/h – 12 km/h** |

---

## 1.4 Primary Classification of Compaction Vehicles

Compaction vehicles are classified by the **mechanism of force application**:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Exam Must-Know Classification:**
> 1. **Static Compactors** (weight-based)
>    - Smooth-Wheel (Static) Rollers
>    - Sheep-Foot / Padfoot Rollers
>    - Pneumatic-Tyred Rollers
> 2. **Dynamic/Vibratory Compactors**
>    - Vibratory Smooth Drum Rollers
>    - Vibratory Padfoot Rollers
>    - Tandem Vibratory Rollers
> 3. **Impact/Ramming Compactors**
>    - Rammers (Trench Compactors)
>    - Vibrating Plate Compactors
>    - Jumping Jack Compactors
> 4. **Combination Compactors**
>    - Soil–Asphalt Combinations
>    - Multi-purpose rollers

---

## 1.5 Real-World Applications & Use Cases

| Compactor Type | Best Suited For | Typical Project |
|---|---|---|
| Smooth-Wheel Roller | Granular soils, crushed rock, asphalt finishing | Highway asphalt layers |
| Sheep-Foot / Padfoot | Cohesive soils (clay), embankments | Earth dam construction |
| Pneumatic-Tyred | Both cohesive & granular, finishing | Sub-base, base course |
| Vibratory Smooth Drum | Granular soils, asphalt | Road base, airport runways |
| Vibratory Padfoot | Semi-cohesive soils | Embankment fills |
| Rammer / Trench Compactor | Confined narrow areas | Pipeline trenches, backfills |
| Plate Compactor | Small patches, narrow zones | Footpaths, paver bedding |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Mechanism of Compaction — Why Compaction Works

Compaction densifies a soil mass through **three simultaneous physical processes**:

### Step 1: Rearrangement (Particle Reorientation)
Soil grains roll and slide past one another, moving from a loose, open structure into a tighter, denser packing arrangement. This dominates in **granular soils** (sand, gravel).

### Step 2: Deformation (Elastic + Plastic Bending)
Under sustained load, soil particles deform slightly at contact points, increasing contact area and reducing stress concentration. This is most pronounced in **cohesive soils** (clay).

### Step 3: Fracture (Crushing of Grains)
At very high contact pressures, brittle grains fracture and fill the voids. This is undesirable in aggregates but occurs naturally in **rock fills** during heavy compaction.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** Each soil type responds best to a specific compaction mechanism. Granular soils need **vibration**; cohesive soils need **pressure (kneading)**; mixed soils need **combined loading**.

---

## 2.2 Types of Compaction Forces Explained

### A. Static Weight Compaction
$$F_s = M \cdot g$$

The compactor's dead weight presses down on the soil. Best for **shallow lifts** of well-graded material.

### B. Dynamic / Impact Compaction
$$F_i = M \cdot (a + g)$$

Where:
- $a$ = deceleration during drum impact
- $g$ = gravitational acceleration

Used in rammers where a heavy plate is repeatedly dropped or accelerated downward.

### C. Vibratory Compaction (Centrifugal Force)
$$F_c = m_e \cdot \omega^2 \cdot r$$

Where:
- $m_e$ = eccentric mass (kg)
- $\omega$ = angular velocity (rad/s) = $2\pi f$
- $r$ = eccentricity radius (m)
- $f$ = vibration frequency (Hz)

This force adds to gravity, producing an **effective dynamic force** that rapidly cycles the soil structure, allowing particles to settle.

### D. Kneading Action (Pneumatic Tyred Roller)
The flexible tires create a **wavelike pressure front** that travels through the soil, producing both static pressure and lateral shear — a kneading effect ideal for **clayey soils**.

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memorize these formulas — they are KTU exam favourites.**

| # | Formula | Description | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $F_c = m_e \cdot \omega^2 \cdot r$ | Centrifugal force from eccentric mass | N |
| 2 | $\omega = 2\pi f$ | Angular velocity from frequency | rad/s |
| 3 | $F_{total} = F_s + F_c = Mg + m_e\omega^2 r$ | Total applied compaction force | N |
| 4 | $CF = \dfrac{F_c}{M g}$ | Centrifugal factor (ratio of dynamic to static weight) | dimensionless |
| 5 | $A = \dfrac{m_e \cdot r}{M}$ | Nominal amplitude (single amplitude) | m |
| 6 | $a = A \cdot \omega^2$ | Peak drum acceleration | m/s² |
| 7 | $q_l = \dfrac{M g}{W}$ | Static linear load | N/m |
| 8 | $CF_{total} = \dfrac{(M g + F_c)}{M g}$ | Total compaction factor including gravity | dimensionless |
| 9 | $N = \dfrac{v \cdot f}{s}$ | Number of passes per unit area (depends on travel speed) | passes/m |

> **Engineering Rule of Thumb (KTU Board Tip):**
> A **Centrifugal Factor (CF)** between **1.5 and 4.0** is optimal. Below 1.5, the vibratory effect is too weak. Above 4.5, the drum begins to "bounce" off the surface, losing coupling.

---

## 2.4 Specifications of Compaction Vehicles — The Eight Critical Parameters

A KTU examiner may ask you to specify a compactor for a given job. Always evaluate these **eight parameters**:

1. **Operating Mass (kg)** — heavier mass = higher static compaction.
2. **Drum Dimensions** — diameter and width affect contact area and pressure.
3. **Frequency (Hz)** — controls the rate of cyclic loading.
4. **Amplitude (mm)** — controls the depth of vibration penetration.
5. **Centrifugal Force (kN)** — measures vibratory intensity.
6. **Static Linear Load (kg/cm)** — pressure under the drum.
7. **Travel Speed (km/h)** — affects number of passes and productivity.
8. **Drum Type** — smooth, padfoot, or pneumatic; matched to soil type.

---

## 2.5 Engineering Utility — Where Compaction Vehicles Are Used in Industry

| Sector | Application |
|---|---|
| **Highway Engineering** | Subgrade, sub-base, base course, bituminous layers |
| **Railway Engineering** | Track ballast densification, formation layer |
| **Dam Construction** | Earth-fill dam embankments, clay cores |
| **Airport Runways** | Pavement layers, taxiways, aprons |
| **Urban Infrastructure** | Backfill around foundations, trench backfilling |
| **Landfill Operations** | Solid waste compaction, daily cover |
| **Industrial Yards** | Container terminals, storage yards, parking lots |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation of Centrifugal Force Generated by Eccentric Mass

> **Starting Concept:** Inside every vibratory roller drum, an **eccentric shaft** rotates at a fixed speed. The unbalanced mass on the shaft creates a rotating centrifugal force vector.

### Given:
- $m_e$ = unbalanced eccentric mass (kg)
- $r$ = eccentricity radius (distance from shaft center to center of mass) (m)
- $n$ = shaft rotational speed (rev/min or rpm)
- $f$ = vibration frequency in Hz

### Step 1: Convert Rotational Speed to Angular Velocity
The rotational speed in rpm must first be converted to angular velocity in rad/s.

$$\omega = \frac{2\pi \cdot n}{60}$$

### Step 2: Express the Rotating Centrifugal Force Vector
A rotating mass produces a force vector that points radially outward from the shaft axis.

$$F_c = m_e \cdot \omega^2 \cdot r$$

### Step 3: Substitute $\omega$ in Terms of Frequency
Using $\omega = 2\pi f$:

$$\omega^2 = (2\pi f)^2 = 4\pi^2 f^2$$

Therefore:

$$F_c = m_e \cdot 4\pi^2 f^2 \cdot r$$

### Step 4: Final Compact Form

$$\boxed{F_c = 4 \pi^2 \, m_e \, f^2 \, r}$$

> **Engineering Insight:** Notice that $F_c$ depends **quadratically on frequency** $f$. Doubling the rotational speed **quadruples** the centrifugal force. This is why frequency is the most powerful control parameter in a vibratory compactor.

---

## 3.2 Derivation of Total Compaction Force

The total downward force experienced by the soil combines gravity and centrifugal components.

### Given:
- Static weight contribution = $M \cdot g$
- Dynamic centrifugal contribution = $m_e \cdot \omega^2 \cdot r$

### Step 1: Identify the Two Force Vectors

The static weight $M g$ always acts vertically downward. The centrifugal force $F_c$ rotates, so its vertical component varies sinusoidally, but its peak downward contribution equals its peak magnitude.

### Step 2: Sum the Peak Forces

At the moment the eccentric mass is at its lowest position, the centrifugal force adds directly to the static weight.

$$F_{peak} = M \cdot g + m_e \cdot \omega^2 \cdot r$$

### Step 3: Define the Total Compaction Factor

The compaction factor (CF) expresses the ratio of dynamic to static force.

$$CF = \frac{F_c}{M g} = \frac{m_e \cdot \omega^2 \cdot r}{M \cdot g}$$

The **effective peak force ratio** then becomes:

$$CF_{peak} = \frac{M g + F_c}{M g} = 1 + \frac{m_e \omega^2 r}{M g}$$

> **Practical Note:** A compactor with a CF between 1.5 and 4.0 is classified as **vibratory**. Below 1.5, the machine behaves like a static roller. Above 4.5, the drum jumps ("bouncing mode"), and compaction efficiency drops sharply.

---

## 3.3 Derivation of Nominal Amplitude

The amplitude of vibration describes how far the drum (and the eccentric mechanism inside it) displaces from its mean position.

### Given:
- $m_e$ = eccentric mass (kg)
- $r$ = eccentricity radius (m)
- $M$ = total vibrating mass (drum + frame + eccentric) (kg)

### Step 1: Write the Mass-Eccentricity Product
The product $m_e \cdot r$ is called the **static moment** of the eccentric (kg·m).

### Step 2: Relate Static Moment to Vibration Amplitude
For a vibrating body supported on elastic elements (drum mounts/isolators), the peak displacement amplitude is:

$$A = \frac{m_e \cdot r}{M}$$

### Step 3: Final Form

$$\boxed{A = \frac{m_e \, r}{M}} \quad \text{(in meters, convert to mm for engineering use)}$$

> **Typical KTU Reference Values:**
> - **Low amplitude:** 0.4–0.8 mm — used on asphalt and thin layers.
> - **High amplitude:** 1.5–2.0 mm — used on thick granular lifts (300–500 mm).

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Specifying a Compactor by Soil Type

This matrix is the **single most important KTU exam table** for Module 4.

| Soil Type | Particle Size | Cohesion | Best Compactor Type | Frequency | Amplitude |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Sand & Gravel** | Coarse (> 75 µm) | Low (non-cohesive) | Vibratory smooth-drum | High (40–80 Hz) | Low (0.4–0.8 mm) |
| **Silty Sand** | Medium | Low–medium | Vibratory smooth or pneumatic | Medium (30–45 Hz) | Medium (0.8–1.2 mm) |
| **Sandy Clay** | Mixed | Medium | Vibratory padfoot or pneumatic | Medium (25–35 Hz) | Medium (1.0–1.5 mm) |
| **Clay (heavy)** | Fine (< 2 µm) | High (cohesive) | Sheepsfoot / Padfoot | Low (20–30 Hz) | High (1.5–2.0 mm) |
| **Rockfill** | Very coarse | None | Heavy vibratory, 25-ton class | Low (25 Hz) | Very High (2.0+ mm) |
| **Asphalt mix** | Aggregate + binder | Cohesive (binder) | Tandem vibratory | High (40–60 Hz) | Very Low (0.3–0.5 mm) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Logic Behind the Rule:**
> - **Granular soils** = particles move by vibration. Use **high frequency, low amplitude**.
> - **Cohesive soils** = particles move by kneading. Use **low frequency, high amplitude** to push deep.
> - **Asphalt** = need gentle re-arrangement without fracturing aggregates. Use **high frequency, low amplitude**.

---

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example — KTU Board Style

### **Problem:**
A vibratory roller has the following specifications:
- Operating mass $M = 12{,}000$ kg
- Eccentric mass $m_e = 14$ kg
- Eccentricity radius $r = 0.25$ m
- Vibration frequency $f = 30$ Hz

**Calculate:**
1. Centrifugal force $F_c$
2. Total peak force $F_{peak}$
3. Compaction factor $CF$
4. Nominal amplitude $A$

### **Step 1: Centrifugal Force**

Using $F_c = 4 \pi^2 \, m_e \, f^2 \, r$:

$$F_c = 4 \cdot \pi^2 \cdot 14 \cdot (30)^2 \cdot 0.25$$

$$F_c = 4 \cdot 9.8696 \cdot 14 \cdot 900 \cdot 0.25$$

$$F_c = 124{,}215 \text{ N} \approx 124.2 \text{ kN}$$

### **Step 2: Total Peak Force**

Static weight:

$$M g = 12{,}000 \cdot 9.81 = 117{,}720 \text{ N} = 117.72 \text{ kN}$$

Total peak force:

$$F_{peak} = M g + F_c = 117.72 + 124.22 = 241.94 \text{ kN}$$

### **Step 3: Compaction Factor**

$$CF = \frac{F_c}{M g} = \frac{124.22}{117.72} = 1.055$$

This value is **below 1.5**, indicating that the compactor would behave predominantly as a static roller — to improve, the frequency or eccentric mass would need to be increased.

### **Step 4: Nominal Amplitude**

$$A = \frac{m_e \cdot r}{M} = \frac{14 \cdot 0.25}{12{,}000} = 2.917 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m} = 0.292 \text{ mm}$$

This amplitude is in the **low-amplitude range**, suitable for thin-layer asphalt compaction.

> [!NOTE]
> **Conclusion:** The compactor is well-suited for **finishing work on asphalt layers** but should not be selected for deep granular lifts, where amplitude > 1.0 mm would be required.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Force Generation Inside a Vibratory Roller Drum

The following functional architecture flow maps how a vibratory compactor's drum converts engine power into the compaction force applied to the soil.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Diesel Engine] --> B[Hydraulic Pump]
    B --> C[Hydraulic Motor]
    C --> D[Vibratory Drive Shaft]
    D --> E[Eccentric Mass Rotor]
    E --> F[Centrifugal Force Generation]
    F --> G[Drum Surface]
    G --> H[Ground Soil Layer]
    H --> I[Densified Soil Output]
    J[Operator Control Panel] --> C
    K[Frame and Vibration Isolators] --> G
    J --> L[Frequency Selector 25 to 80 Hz]
    L --> C
```

**Reading the Flow:**

1. The diesel engine powers a hydraulic pump.
2. The pump feeds a hydraulic motor connected to a drive shaft inside the drum.
3. The drive shaft carries eccentric masses that rotate at controlled speed.
4. The rotation creates a **centrifugal force** vector that acts on the drum.
5. The drum transmits this combined static + dynamic force into the soil.
6. **Vibration isolators** between the drum and the machine frame protect the operator from vibration.
7. The **operator control panel** sets the desired frequency to match the soil type.

---

## 4.2 Classification Flowchart — Compaction Vehicle Selection Logic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Compaction Vehicle] --> B{Soil Type}
    B -->|Granular| C{Vibration Needed}
    B -->|Cohesive| D{Depth of Lift}
    B -->|Mixed| E[Combination Roller]
    C -->|Yes| F[Vibratory Smooth Drum Roller]
    C -->|No| G[Static Smooth Drum Roller]
    D -->|Shallow| H[Pneumatic Tyred Roller]
    D -->|Deep| I[Sheep Foot Padfoot Roller]
    F --> J{Asphalt or Soil}
    J -->|Asphalt| K[Tandem Vibratory Roller]
    J -->|Soil| L[Single Drum Vibratory]
    E --> M[Pneumatic Tyred]
    E --> N[Multi Tyre Configuration]
```

**Selection Rule:**

- Start by identifying the **soil type** (granular vs cohesive vs mixed).
- Then decide on **lift thickness** and **whether vibration is permissible** (e.g., near buildings, vibration may be restricted).
- Finally, choose between **single drum vs tandem drum** based on whether asphalt is being compacted.

---

## 4.3 Functional Block Diagram — Vibratory Drum Cross-Section

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph DRUM[Vibratory Drum Cross Section]
        A1[Outer Drum Shell] --> A2[Drum Mounting Flange]
        A2 --> A3[Drive Shaft with Bearings]
        A3 --> A4[Eccentric Mass 1]
        A3 --> A5[Eccentric Mass 2]
        A4 --> A6[Centrifugal Force Vector]
        A5 --> A6
        A6 --> A7[Vibration Isolator to Frame]
    end
    DRUM --> SOIL[Soil Below Drum]
    SOIL --> COMPACT[Densification and Particle Rearrangement]
```

**Interpretation:**

Two eccentric masses mounted 180° apart on the same shaft create a **self-balancing vibration**. Their centrifugal forces cancel each other in the horizontal direction but add in the vertical direction, producing a vertical "pounding" force on the soil.

---

## 4.4 Compaction Mechanism Comparison Matrix (Block Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Compaction Action] --> B[Static Weight]
    A --> C[Dynamic Vibration]
    A --> D[Impact Ramming]
    A --> E[Kneading Pneumatic]
    B --> B1[Constant pressure under drum]
    C --> C1[Oscillatory force via eccentric mass]
    D --> D1[Sudden impulse via dropping weight]
    E --> E1[Tire pressure creates wave front in soil]
    B1 --> SOIL[Effective Compaction of Soil]
    C1 --> SOIL
    D1 --> SOIL
    E1 --> SOIL
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**Define a compaction vehicle. List the four major categories of compaction equipment based on the mechanism of force application.**

#### **Model Answer:**

A **compaction vehicle** is a heavy construction machine designed to densify soil, aggregate, asphalt, or waste material through the application of static weight, vibration, impact, or kneading action, with the goal of increasing density, reducing voids, and improving load-bearing capacity.

**The four major categories are:**
1. **Static Compactors** — e.g., smooth-wheel rollers, sheep-foot rollers
2. **Vibratory (Dynamic) Compactors** — e.g., vibratory smooth-drum rollers, tandem rollers
3. **Impact / Ramming Compactors** — e.g., rammers, jumping-jacks, plate compactors
4. **Pneumatic-Tyred (Kneading) Compactors** — e.g., multi-wheel pneumatic rollers

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Explain the significance of "Centrifugal Force" in a vibratory roller. How is it generated?**

#### **Model Answer:**

**Significance:** Centrifugal force is the dynamic force generated inside a vibratory drum by an **eccentric rotating mass**. It adds to the static weight of the drum and creates cyclic pressure pulses that rearrange soil particles into a denser configuration.

**Generation:** Inside the drum, a shaft carries an unbalanced mass (the eccentric mass) at a radius $r$ from the axis. When the shaft rotates at frequency $f$, the unbalanced mass produces a centrifugal force:

$$F_c = 4\pi^2 \, m_e \, f^2 \, r$$

This rotating force vector, when summed with the static weight $M g$, produces the **effective compaction force** experienced by the soil.

---

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**Part (a) [7 Marks]:**

Explain the working principle and constructional features of a **vibratory smooth-drum roller**. With the help of a labeled sketch, describe how an eccentric mass inside the drum generates a dynamic compaction force.

**Part (b) [7 Marks]:**

A vibratory compactor has the following specifications:
- Operating mass: $M = 10{,}000$ kg
- Eccentric mass: $m_e = 12$ kg
- Eccentricity radius: $r = 0.30$ m
- Vibration frequency: $f = 32$ Hz

Calculate:
1. Centrifugal force
2. Total peak force on the ground
3. Centrifugal Factor
4. Nominal amplitude

---

#### **Model Solution — Part (a):**

A **vibratory smooth-drum roller** consists of the following components:

1. **Diesel Engine** — drives the hydraulic system.
2. **Hydraulic Pump and Motor** — convert engine power into rotary motion.
3. **Vibratory Drum** — a hollow steel cylinder that transmits compaction energy to the ground.
4. **Eccentric Shaft** — mounted inside the drum; carries one or more unbalanced masses.
5. **Vibration Isolators** — rubber/PU mounts that isolate the frame from drum vibrations.
6. **Operator Cabin** — houses the control panel for frequency and amplitude selection.
7. **Articulated Frame** — allows the roller to steer around curves.

**Working Principle:**

The drum is pressed against the ground by the static weight of the machine. Simultaneously, the eccentric mass inside the drum is rotated at high speed by the hydraulic motor. This rotation generates a **centrifugal force** perpendicular to the drum axis. As the eccentric mass rotates, this force vector also rotates, creating a cyclic vertical force that — combined with gravity — produces high-amplitude pressure pulses on the soil. The soil particles momentarily lose inter-particle friction and rearrange into a denser packing.

**Sketch (Description in Words for Exam):**

> Draw a circular drum showing:
> - Outer steel shell
> - Inside: a horizontal shaft with two eccentric masses placed 180° apart
> - Bearings at both ends of the shaft
> - Direction arrow of rotation
> - Resulting downward arrow showing centrifugal force
> - Below the drum, the soil layer with compaction arrows pointing downward.

---

#### **Model Solution — Part (b):**

**Given:**
- $M = 10{,}000$ kg
- $m_e = 12$ kg
- $r = 0.30$ m
- $f = 32$ Hz

**(i) Centrifugal Force:**

$$F_c = 4\pi^2 m_e f^2 r$$

$$F_c = 4 \times \pi^2 \times 12 \times (32)^2 \times 0.30$$

$$F_c = 4 \times 9.8696 \times 12 \times 1024 \times 0.30$$

$$F_c = 145{,}614 \text{ N} \approx 145.6 \text{ kN}$$

**(ii) Total Peak Force:**

$$F_{peak} = M g + F_c = (10{,}000 \times 9.81) + 145{,}614$$

$$F_{peak} = 98{,}100 + 145{,}614 = 243{,}714 \text{ N} \approx 243.7 \text{ kN}$$

**(iii) Centrifugal Factor:**

$$CF = \frac{F_c}{M g} = \frac{145{,}614}{98{,}100} = 1.484$$

**(iv) Nominal Amplitude:**

$$A = \frac{m_e \cdot r}{M} = \frac{12 \times 0.30}{10{,}000} = 3.6 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m} = 0.36 \text{ mm}$$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 — Alternate Module Choice]`

**Part (a) [7 Marks]:**

Compare **smooth-wheel rollers**, **sheep-foot rollers**, and **pneumatic-tyred rollers** in terms of working principle, suitable soil type, layer thickness, and compaction effectiveness. State advantages and disadvantages of each.

**Part (b) [7 Marks]:**

Discuss the selection criteria for choosing between **static and vibratory compaction** for the following applications, justifying each:
1. Earth dam embankment (clay core)
2. Highway base course (granular)
3. Bituminous layer
4. Pipeline trench backfill

---

#### **Model Solution — Part (a):**

| Parameter | Smooth-Wheel Roller | Sheep-Foot Roller | Pneumatic-Tyred Roller |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Working Principle** | Static weight on smooth steel drum | Static weight on drum studded with pad-like projections | Static weight via flexible pneumatic tires (kneading) |
| **Best Soil Type** | Granular, crushed rock, asphalt finish | Cohesive soils (clay), silty clay | Mixed soils, both cohesive and granular |
| **Layer Thickness** | Thin layers (150–300 mm) | Thick layers (200–500 mm) | Medium (150–400 mm) |
| **Contact Pressure** | Very high (linear load) | High at the pad tips | Moderate (variable with tire pressure) |
| **Effectiveness** | Best for surface finish and densification of granular layers | Best for breaking down clay clods, deep compaction | Best for sealing surfaces and producing uniform density |

**Smooth-Wheel Roller:**
- ✅ Smooth surface finish, ideal for asphalt.
- ❌ Poor on cohesive soils (does not knead).

**Sheep-Foot Roller:**
- ✅ Walks on its pads, applying high local pressure; ideal for clay.
- ❌ Cannot finish surfaces (leaves pad marks).

**Pneumatic-Tyred Roller:**
- ✅ Versatile, kneading action seals the surface.
- ❌ Tire wear is high on rocky ground.

---

#### **Model Solution — Part (b):**

**(i) Earth Dam Embankment (Clay Core):**
- **Choice: Static Sheep-Foot Roller (or low-frequency vibratory padfoot).**
- **Justification:** Clay requires kneading action and deep compaction in thick layers. Vibration at high frequency may liquefy the clay. Static pressure from padfoot rollers squeezes the clay from the bottom up, expelling air voids.

**(ii) Highway Base Course (Granular):**
- **Choice: Vibratory Smooth-Drum Roller.**
- **Justification:** Granular soil particles rearrange quickly when vibrated. High frequency and moderate amplitude allow particles to settle into a dense packing. Static rollers cannot achieve the same density in thick granular layers.

**(iii) Bituminous Layer:**
- **Choice: Tandem Vibratory Roller (high frequency, low amplitude).**
- **Justification:** Asphalt mix requires high frequency to vibrate the mix without fracturing aggregates. Low amplitude prevents over-penetration. Static rollers are used only for the final "breakdown" pass.

**(iv) Pipeline Trench Backfill:**
- **Choice: Rammer (trench compactor) or small plate compactor.**
- **Justification:** Trenches are narrow and confined. Large rollers cannot enter. Rammers deliver high-impact force to confined spaces, achieving high density around the pipe without damaging it.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks**
>
> 1. **Confusing "centrifugal force" with "centripetal force"** — they are equal in magnitude but opposite in direction. Centrifugal force is the *apparent* outward force on the rotating mass, used here for engineering convenience.
> 2. **Forgetting to convert rpm to Hz** before applying $F_c = 4\pi^2 m_e f^2 r$.
> 3. **Misclassifying soil types** — students frequently write "clay" for granular soils or vice versa. Memorize: **granular = vibration; cohesive = pressure/kneading.**
> 4. **Skipping units in final answers** — KTU deducts marks if the final answer has no unit (e.g., kN).
> 5. **Drawing only rough sketches without labels** — every part of a labeled diagram carries a mark. Always label **all** components: drum, eccentric shaft, isolator, frame, soil, direction of centrifugal force.

---

## TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — KTU Module 4: Compaction Vehicles**

- ✅ **Compaction vehicle** = machine that densifies soil/asphalt by static, vibratory, impact, or kneading action.
- ✅ **Four categories:** Static, Vibratory, Impact (Ramming), Pneumatic-Tyred (Kneading).
- ✅ **Centrifugal force formula:** $F_c = 4\pi^2 m_e f^2 r$
- ✅ **Angular velocity:** $\omega = 2\pi f$
- ✅ **Total peak force:** $F_{peak} = M g + m_e \omega^2 r$
- ✅ **Compaction factor:** $CF = F_c / (M g)$ — optimal range **1.5 to 4.0**.
- ✅ **Nominal amplitude:** $A = m_e r / M$
- ✅ **Granular soils** → use **high frequency, low amplitude** vibratory rollers.
- ✅ **Cohesive soils** → use **low frequency, high amplitude** or static padfoot rollers.
- ✅ **Asphalt** → use **tandem vibratory, high frequency, low amplitude**.
- ✅ **Confined spaces** → use **rammers or plate compactors**.
- ✅ **Eight specifications** to evaluate any compactor: mass, drum size, frequency, amplitude, $F_c$, $q_l$, travel speed, drum type.
- ✅ **Earth dam** → Sheep-foot / Padfoot (clay core).
- ✅ **Highway base** → Vibratory smooth drum (granular).
- ✅ **Asphalt layer** → Tandem vibratory.
- ✅ **Trench backfill** → Rammer.

**Key Constants for Exam:**
- $g = 9.81$ m/s²
- Optimal $CF = 1.5$ to $4.0$
- Granular compaction frequency: 40–80 Hz
- Cohesive compaction frequency: 20–30 Hz

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Smooth Wheeled Rollers, Pneumatic Tired Rollers, Agricultural Rollers, Sheep's Foot Rollers
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEMUT614 - SPECIAL TYPES OF VEHICLES
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Smooth Wheeled Rollers, Pneumatic Tired Rollers, Agricultural Rollers, Sheep's Foot Rollers

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Introduction to Compaction Equipment

In the domain of **earthwork engineering** and **highway construction**, compaction is the process of applying mechanical energy to a soil mass to **densify it** by expelling air from the void spaces. The performance of pavements, embankments, dams, and runways is critically dependent on the **degree of compaction** achieved during construction. The machines used for this purpose are broadly classified as **compaction rollers**, and this module focuses on the four most important static and pneumatic types.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 4):** This topic covers the classification, working principle, constructional features, and application areas of *four principal types of static rollers*. These constitute the most frequently asked short-answer and 14-mark questions in KTU University examinations on construction equipment.

---

## 1.2 Smooth Wheeled Rollers (Static Dead Weight Rollers)

### Formal Definition
A **Smooth Wheeled Roller** (also called a **Static Tandem Roller** or **Macadam Roller**) is a self-propelled compaction device consisting of one or two smooth-surfaced cylindrical steel drums mounted on a rigid frame. Compaction is achieved purely by the **static dead weight** of the machine transmitted to the ground through line contact of the drums. The contact pressure is typically in the range of **20 to 55 kg/cm²**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Terminology:** The term *static* distinguishes this machine from vibratory rollers — the smooth wheel roller relies **only on gravity-induced weight**, not on dynamic impulses.

### Intuitive Analogy
> [!TIP]
> **Think of it this way:** Imagine rolling a heavy steel kitchen roller (rolling pin) over a slab of dough. The smooth drum simply *presses* the surface down. There is no kneading or punching action. The deeper the weight and the more passes you make, the tighter the surface becomes. This is exactly how a smooth wheel roller works on **granular soils like gravel, sand, or crushed rock**.

### Constructional Features
- **Drums:** One large steel drum at the front (drive) and one at the rear (steer), or a single three-wheel configuration.
- **Ballast Compartment:** Drums can be filled with **water, wet sand, or steel weights** to increase mass and hence contact pressure.
- **Weight Range:** Empty weight of **2 to 15 tonnes**; ballasted weight up to **20 tonnes**.
- **Travel Speed:** Low, typically **2 to 6 km/h**, to allow sufficient time for soil rearrangement.

---

## 1.3 Pneumatic Tired Rollers

### Formal Definition
A **Pneumatic Tired Roller** (also called a **Multi-Wheel Rubber-Tired Roller**) is a compaction machine that uses a **closely spaced array of rubber tires** mounted on one or two axles, operating at a defined **tyre inflation pressure**. Compaction is produced by a combination of the **static weight** of the machine and the **kneading action** caused by the elastic deformation of the pneumatic tyres.

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Parameter — Tyre Pressure:** The contact pressure on the soil is **not equal** to the inflation pressure. It is governed by a complex relationship involving the wheel load, tyre dimensions, ply rating, and inflation pressure. The applied contact pressure typically ranges from **3.5 to 7 kg/cm²**.

### Intuitive Analogy
> [!TIP]
> **The "lawn roller that kneads" analogy:** Unlike a steel drum that flattens, picture pressing your hand with a slightly squishy rubber ball — the ball deforms to grip the surface and produces a combined pressing-and-kneading effect. Now imagine 7 to 13 such rubber "balls" in a row. This kneading seals the surface against water ingress, much like how a baker presses dough to make a tight crust.

### Configuration
- **Front Axle:** Steering axle with 4 wheels (1 row).
- **Rear Axle:** Drive axle with 4 wheels (1 row).
- **Total Wheels:** **7 to 13 wheels** (with a smaller middle wheel for offset coverage so that no strip is missed).
- **Tyre Pressure Variation:** Inflation pressure can be varied between **3.5 kg/cm² (50 psi)** and **10.5 kg/cm² (150 psi)** depending on soil type.

---

## 1.4 Agricultural Rollers

### Formal Definition
An **Agricultural Roller** (or **Agricultural Disc Harrow Roller**) is a specialized compaction implement used primarily in **farmland preparation, sodding operations, and light embankment works**. It typically consists of a **series of cast-iron discs or smooth rollers** mounted on a common axle, often towed behind a tractor.

> [!NOTE]
> **Application Context:** Although covered in the KTU syllabus under construction equipment, agricultural rollers find their primary use in **land leveling, pasture management, and breaking up clods** rather than in high-specification highway compaction.

### Intuitive Analogy
> [!TIP]
> **The "garden roller" analogy:** Picture the small, heavy cylindrical roller that groundskeepers use to flatten cricket pitches and golf greens. Agricultural rollers are the larger, tractor-towed cousins of this device, often with a corrugated or ribbed surface for a light tilling-plus-compaction effect.

### Key Variants
- **Smooth Surface Agricultural Roller:** Plain cast-iron drum, used for final flattening.
- **Crumbler Roller:** Surface is ribbed or notched, providing a mild soil-pulverizing action.
- **Weight Range:** Typically **0.5 to 5 tonnes**, filled with water for additional ballast.

---

## 1.5 Sheep's Foot Rollers

### Formal Definition
A **Sheep's Foot Roller** (also called a **Padfoot Roller** or **Tamping Roller**) is a compaction machine whose drum is studded with a large number of **protruding feet (or "tamping pads")** shaped like a sheep's hoof. The pads have a small contact area (typically **40 to 100 cm² each**), which creates an **extremely high local contact pressure** of **30 to 100 kg/cm²** even though the overall machine weight is moderate.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why the "Sheep's Foot" name?** The shape of each individual projecting pad is designed to mimic the hoof of a sheep — small, blunt, and capable of penetrating the soil surface. The kneading action generated by these feet is what makes this roller uniquely suited for **cohesive soils (clays and silts)**.

### Intuitive Analogy
> [!TIP]
> **The "meat tenderizer" analogy:** A smooth wheel roller is like pressing a flat plate onto a piece of meat — it only flattens the top surface. A sheep's foot roller is like using a meat tenderizer with dozens of sharp metal prongs — the prongs penetrate deep, breaking the internal bonds, and the resulting high local pressure compacts the soil from within. The pads literally "walk out" of the soil as compaction progresses, indicating that the desired density is being reached.

### Standard Specifications
- **Pad Foot Projections:** Typically **90 to 120 feet per square meter** of drum surface.
- **Pad Area:** **30 to 80 cm²** per foot.
- **Drum Diameter:** **1.0 to 1.8 m**.
- **Contact Pressure:** **15 to 75 kg/cm²** (varies with ballasting).
- **Operating Speed:** Very low, **2 to 4 km/h**, to allow penetration.

---

## 1.6 Comparative Overview at a Glance

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Selection Rule of Thumb (KTU Viva Favorite):**
> - **Granular Soils (Gravel, Sand)** → **Smooth Wheeled Roller**
> - **Mixed Soils (Silty Sand, Clayey Gravel)** → **Pneumatic Tired Roller**
> - **Cohesive Soils (Clay, Silt)** → **Sheep's Foot Roller**
> - **Surface Finishing & Farmland** → **Agricultural Roller**
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Basis of Compaction

Compaction can be explained through three fundamental mechanisms, each produced by a different roller type:

| Mechanism | Physical Process | Roller Type | Best Suited Soil |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Static Pressure** | Direct vertical stress expels air | Smooth Wheeled Roller | Granular, non-cohesive |
| **Kneading Action** | Cyclic shear from tyre flex | Pneumatic Tired Roller | Mixed / silty soils |
| **Manipulation / Tamping** | Localized high pressure with penetration | Sheep's Foot Roller | Cohesive clay, silt |
| **Smoothing / Light Pressing** | Surface finish with mild tilling | Agricultural Roller | Topsoil, farmland |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why different rollers for different soils?** Cohesive soils have **electro-chemical bonds** between particles. They resist compaction under simple static pressure but yield to **sustained kneading** or **localized shearing** (sheep's foot). Granular soils, on the other hand, rearrange under direct compressive stress (smooth wheel).

---

## 2.2 Smooth Wheeled Roller — Operating Theory

### Working Principle
1. The **total dead weight (W)** of the machine is transmitted through the **line contact** between the steel drum and the soil.
2. The contact area is approximated as a rectangle of length **L** (drum width) and width equal to the **chord of contact (b)**.
3. The **contact pressure (p)** is computed using:

$$ p = \frac{W_{drum}}{L \times b} $$

where:
- $W_{drum}$ = weight on a single drum (kg or N)
- $L$ = drum width (cm)
- $b$ = chord of contact (cm)

### Engineering Interpretation
- For a steel drum on a soft soil, **b** can be as large as **15 to 25 cm**, which **lowers** the contact pressure despite the high machine weight.
- Hence, **smooth wheel rollers are inefficient on soft, deep lifts** — the contact chord widens before the soil can be densified.
- They are most effective in the **top 15 to 30 cm** of a granular layer.
- **Number of Passes Required:** Typically **6 to 10 passes** to reach 95% Proctor density.

---

## 2.3 Pneumatic Tired Roller — Operating Theory

### Working Principle
1. The total machine weight is distributed over **N** tyres, each carrying an average wheel load of:

$$ W_{wheel} = \frac{W_{machine}}{N} $$

2. The **contact area (A)** under each tyre is determined by the **tyre inflation pressure** and the **ply rating**. Empirically:

$$ A \approx \frac{W_{wheel}}{p_i} $$

where $p_i$ is the **tyre inflation pressure** (kg/cm²).

3. The **mean contact pressure (p_m)** is approximately equal to the inflation pressure:

$$ p_m \approx (0.8 \text{ to } 1.0) \times p_i $$

4. The **kneading action** arises because the front and rear tyres are **offset** (wobbly-wheel or wobbletread arrangement), so they do not travel in the same track. This produces a **horizontal shearing deformation** in the top layer of soil.

### Key Engineering Parameters
- **Tyre Spacing:** 4 to 6 inches between adjacent tyre edges to ensure uniform coverage.
- **Wobble-Wheel Feature:** Rear axle tyres can be oscillated horizontally to provide full-surface coverage.
- **Tyre Pressure Adjustment:** Lowering pressure **increases contact area** and **decreases contact pressure** — useful for soft, recently placed layers.

### Maximum Wheel Load vs. Inflation Pressure
| Tyre Ply Rating | Inflation Pressure (kg/cm²) | Max Wheel Load (kg) |
|---|---|---|
| 6 PR | 3.5 | 1,000 |
| 10 PR | 5.6 | 1,800 |
| 14 PR | 7.7 | 2,500 |
| 18 PR | 10.5 | 3,600 |

---

## 2.4 Agricultural Roller — Operating Theory

### Working Principle
- Agricultural rollers are **tractor-towed (or self-propelled) implements** consisting of a hollow steel drum that can be **ballasted with water**.
- The compaction is by **static pressure only** but at a **much lower intensity** than construction rollers.
- The corrugated/ribbed variants provide **mild pulverization** of soil clods — useful in seedbed preparation.

### Engineering Parameters
- **Empty Weight:** 500 to 1,500 kg.
- **Water-Ballasted Weight:** 1,500 to 5,000 kg.
- **Drum Diameter:** 0.6 to 1.2 m.
- **Drum Length:** 1.5 to 3.0 m.
- **Operating Speed:** 4 to 8 km/h.

---

## 2.5 Sheep's Foot Roller — Operating Theory

### Working Principle
1. The drum is studded with **tamping pads** (sheep's feet), each having a small contact area $A_p$.
2. The total number of pads in contact with the soil at any instant is $n$.
3. The **average contact pressure on each foot** is:

$$ p_{foot} = \frac{W_{drum}}{n \times A_p} $$

4. This produces a **very high localized pressure** that **penetrates** the soil, breaking clods and expelling air voids.
5. As compaction proceeds, the **feet "walk out"** of the soil — i.e., the depth of penetration reduces. This is the **field indicator of completion of compaction**.

### Engineering Parameters
- **Pad Area $A_p$:** 30 to 80 cm²
- **Number of Pads:** 90 to 120 per m²
- **Drum Weight (empty):** 5 to 15 tonnes
- **Drum Weight (ballasted):** Up to 30 tonnes
- **Operating Speed:** 2 to 4 km/h (very slow for penetration)
- **Lift Thickness:** 15 to 30 cm per layer (clay); up to 50 cm for silty clay

### The "Walk-Out" Phenomenon
> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Walk-Out Criterion:** When the roller feet no longer sink into the soil surface by more than **25 to 50 mm** after a pass, the compaction is considered complete. This is a **qualitative field test** used universally on KTU lab visits and site inspections.

---

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Save this table for last-minute revision.** It contains every numerical relationship KTU examiners expect for this topic.

| # | Formula / Concept | Symbol | Expression | Units | Used In |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Contact pressure (smooth wheel) | $p$ | $p = \dfrac{W_{drum}}{L \times b}$ | kg/cm² | Smooth Wheel |
| 2 | Wheel load (pneumatic) | $W_{wheel}$ | $W_{wheel} = \dfrac{W_{machine}}{N}$ | kg | Pneumatic |
| 3 | Contact area (pneumatic) | $A$ | $A \approx \dfrac{W_{wheel}}{p_i}$ | cm² | Pneumatic |
| 4 | Mean contact pressure (pneumatic) | $p_m$ | $p_m \approx (0.8 \text{ to } 1.0)\,p_i$ | kg/cm² | Pneumatic |
| 5 | Foot contact pressure (sheep's foot) | $p_{foot}$ | $p_{foot} = \dfrac{W_{drum}}{n \times A_p}$ | kg/cm² | Sheep's Foot |
| 6 | Compactive effort | $CE$ | $CE = \dfrac{N \times W \times S}{V}$ | kg·m/m³ | General |
| 7 | Optimum moisture content | $OMC$ | Proctor / Modified Proctor | % | Soil Test |
| 8 | Relative compaction | $RC$ | $RC = \dfrac{\gamma_{d,field}}{\gamma_{d,max}} \times 100$ | % | Quality Check |
| 9 | Lift thickness (granular) | — | 20–30 cm | cm | Smooth Wheel |
| 10 | Lift thickness (cohesive) | — | 15–25 cm | cm | Sheep's Foot |
| 11 | Typical operating speed (smooth) | $v$ | 2–6 km/h | km/h | Smooth Wheel |
| 12 | Typical operating speed (sheep's) | $v$ | 2–4 km/h | km/h | Sheep's Foot |
| 13 | Number of passes (smooth) | $N$ | 6–10 | — | Smooth Wheel |
| 14 | Number of passes (pneumatic) | $N$ | 4–8 | — | Pneumatic |
| 15 | Walk-out depth criterion | $d$ | $d \le 25 \text{ to } 50$ mm | mm | Sheep's Foot |

> [!NOTE]
> **Boundary Conditions / Constraints to Remember:**
> - Smooth wheel rollers **must NOT** be used on wet clay — they will **bridge over** the soft surface and fail to densify the lower layer.
> - Sheep's foot rollers **must NOT** be used on clean granular soils — the feet will **dislodge particles** instead of compacting them.
> - Pneumatic rollers are **versatile** but require the **tyre pressure** to be matched to the lift stiffness to avoid rutting.
> - Agricultural rollers are **not for high-specification highway subgrades** — they are limited to surface finishing.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Contact Pressure Under a Smooth Wheel Drum

### Problem Setup
A smooth wheel roller has the following specifications:
- Total machine weight: **W = 8,000 kg**
- Weight distribution: 50% on front drum, 50% on rear drum
- Drum width: **L = 1.5 m = 150 cm**
- Contact chord length: **b = 20 cm** (assumed)

**Find:** The contact pressure $p$ on the soil.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1: Compute the weight on a single drum.**

Since the load is distributed equally between the front and rear drums, the weight on each drum is:

$$ W_{drum} = \frac{W}{2} = \frac{8{,}000}{2} = 4{,}000 \text{ kg} $$

**Step 2: Compute the contact area under the drum.**

The contact patch is rectangular with dimensions $L$ (drum width) and $b$ (chord of contact):

$$ A_{contact} = L \times b = 150 \times 20 = 3{,}000 \text{ cm}^2 $$

**Step 3: Compute the contact pressure.**

$$ p = \frac{W_{drum}}{A_{contact}} = \frac{4{,}000}{3{,}000} = 1.333 \text{ kg/cm}^2 $$

**Step 4: Convert to SI units (kPa) for completeness.**

$$ 1 \text{ kg/cm}^2 = 98.066 \text{ kPa} $$

$$ p = 1.333 \times 98.066 = 130.7 \text{ kPa} $$

**Conversion Logic Explanation:**
- Step 1: Equal load distribution is a **standard assumption** for tandem rollers; always check whether the problem specifies an asymmetric weight distribution.
- Step 2: The contact area approximation (rectangle $L \times b$) is valid for **rigid drums on cohesive soil**. For soft soil, the chord $b$ widens and the contact area becomes elliptical, lowering the actual pressure.
- Step 3: Direct application of **pressure = force / area** in CGS units.
- Step 4: Conversion factor **1 kg/cm² = 98.066 kPa** comes from $1 \text{ kgf/cm}^2 = 9.8066 \text{ N/cm}^2 \times 10 \text{ cm}^2/\text{m}^2 \times 10^{-3} \text{ kN/N} = 98.066 \text{ kPa}$.

**Result:** The contact pressure is approximately **1.33 kg/cm² (130.7 kPa)**.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Note:** This value is **below the 20–55 kg/cm²** range typical of large tandem rollers. The discrepancy arises because the example uses a small roller with a wide contact chord. For a 12-tonne ballasted roller, $b$ would shrink to **5 cm**, giving $p = 5.33 \text{ kg/cm}^2$ — within the textbook range.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Foot Pressure of a Sheep's Foot Roller

### Problem Setup
A sheep's foot roller has:
- Drum weight (ballasted): **W_drum = 12,000 kg**
- Number of pads in contact at any instant: **n = 80**
- Pad area: **A_p = 50 cm²**

**Find:** The average contact pressure on each foot.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1: Recall the governing equation.**

$$ p_{foot} = \frac{W_{drum}}{n \times A_p} $$

**Step 2: Substitute the numerical values.**

$$ p_{foot} = \frac{12{,}000}{80 \times 50} = \frac{12{,}000}{4{,}000} = 3.0 \text{ kg/cm}^2 $$

**Step 3: Convert to SI units.**

$$ p_{foot} = 3.0 \times 98.066 = 294.2 \text{ kPa} $$

**Step 4: Compare with smooth wheel roller pressure.**

For the same drum weight distributed as a smooth wheel with $L = 1.5$ m and $b = 10$ cm:

$$ p_{smooth} = \frac{12{,}000}{150 \times 10} = 8.0 \text{ kg/cm}^2 $$

**Analysis:** Although the smooth wheel has higher mean pressure, the **sheep's foot concentrates stress locally** in narrow columns, producing **deep penetration and internal compaction**, which is more effective for cohesive soils.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conversion Logic Recap:** $1 \text{ kg/cm}^2 \approx 98 \text{ kPa}$. Memorize this constant; KTU numerical problems frequently require kPa answers.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Tyre Contact Pressure for a Pneumatic Roller

### Problem Setup
A pneumatic tired roller has:
- Total machine weight: **W = 20,000 kg**
- Number of tyres: **N = 9** (4 front + 4 rear + 1 middle wobble wheel)
- Tyre inflation pressure: **p_i = 6.0 kg/cm²**
- Empirical ratio: **p_m / p_i = 0.9**

**Find:** The mean contact pressure and the contact area per tyre.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1: Compute the wheel load per tyre.**

$$ W_{wheel} = \frac{W}{N} = \frac{20{,}000}{9} = 2{,}222.22 \text{ kg} $$

**Step 2: Compute the mean contact pressure.**

$$ p_m = 0.9 \times p_i = 0.9 \times 6.0 = 5.4 \text{ kg/cm}^2 $$

**Step 3: Compute the contact area per tyre.**

$$ A = \frac{W_{wheel}}{p_m} = \frac{2{,}222.22}{5.4} \approx 411.5 \text{ cm}^2 $$

**Step 4: Convert to cm² of contact per tyre to m².**

$$ A = 411.5 \text{ cm}^2 = 411.5 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^2 = 0.04115 \text{ m}^2 $$

**Step 5: Equivalently, the contact patch approximates a rectangle of, say, 35 cm × 12 cm.**

Check: $35 \times 12 = 420$ cm² ≈ 411.5 cm² ✓ (consistent)

**Conversion Logic:**
- Step 1: Assumes **uniform loading** across all tyres (wobble wheel carries a slightly different load in real life but is neglected in KTU problems).
- Step 2: The 0.9 factor accounts for the **stiffness of the tyre carcass**; actual contact pressure is slightly **less** than inflation pressure because the sidewall bulges.
- Step 3: Direct application of $A = F / p$ in CGS units.
- Step 4: Unit conversion: $1 \text{ m}^2 = 10{,}000 \text{ cm}^2$, so $1 \text{ cm}^2 = 10^{-4} \text{ m}^2$.
- Step 5: Validation of the result against a physically realistic tyre footprint (35 cm long × 12 cm wide).

**Result:** Mean contact pressure = **5.4 kg/cm² (≈ 530 kPa)**, contact area = **411.5 cm² per tyre**.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Roller Selection Based on Soil Type

| Soil Type | Description | Best Roller | Reason |
|---|---|---|---|
| **GW, GP** (Well-graded / Poorly-graded gravel) | Coarse, free-draining | **Smooth Wheel** | Particles rearrange easily under static pressure |
| **SW, SP** (Well-graded / Poorly-graded sand) | Granular, low cohesion | **Smooth Wheel** or **Vibratory** | Direct stress densifies; vibratory aids particle reorientation |
| **SM** (Silty sand) | Some fines, slight cohesion | **Pneumatic Tired** | Kneading action mobilizes particle rearrangement |
| **SC** (Clayey sand) | Mixed | **Pneumatic Tired** | Surface sealing from tyres; kneading |
| **ML, CL** (Silt, low-plasticity clay) | Cohesive-dominant | **Sheep's Foot** | Feet penetrate, breaking clods |
| **MH, CH** (High-plasticity silt/clay) | Highly cohesive | **Sheep's Foot** + **Pneumatic** (for sealing) | Foot compacts inside; pneumatic seals the surface |
| **Topsoil, farmland** | Loose, organic | **Agricultural Roller** | Light surface pressing, no high-spec needed |

> [!TIP]
> **Exam Tip:** In KTU descriptive questions, always state the **soil class as per IS 1498 (Soil Classification)** alongside the roller recommendation. Examiners reward classification awareness with 1–2 bonus marks.

---

## 3.5 Derivation 4: Compactive Effort Relationship

### Concept
**Compactive Effort (CE)** is a measure of the mechanical energy delivered per unit volume of soil and is defined as:

$$ CE = \frac{N \times W \times S}{V} $$

where:
- $N$ = number of passes
- $W$ = weight of roller (kg)
- $S$ = average travel speed per pass (m)
- $V$ = volume of soil compacted (m³)

### Worked Example
A roller of weight **W = 10,000 kg** makes **N = 8 passes** at an average speed of **S = 100 m per pass** over a compacted volume of **V = 200 m³**.

$$ CE = \frac{8 \times 10{,}000 \times 100}{200} = \frac{8{,}000{,}000}{200} = 40{,}000 \text{ kg·m/m}^3 $$

**Interpretation:** Higher compactive effort → higher achievable dry density, but only up to a saturation point. Beyond that, additional passes are wasteful and may over-stress the soil.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** Students often confuse **compactive effort** (which is a measure of energy input) with **relative compaction** (which is a quality check). KTU questions frequently ask for both — do not interchange them.

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Smooth Wheeled Roller — Functional Architecture

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Engine Power Unit] --> B[Transmission Gearbox]
    B --> C[Front Drum Drive Shaft]
    B --> D[Rear Drum Drive Shaft]
    C --> E[Front Smooth Steel Drum]
    D --> F[Rear Smooth Steel Drum]
    E --> G[Ballast Compartment - Water/Sand]
    F --> G
    E --> H[Operator Cabin and Steering]
    F --> H
    H --> I[Contact Patch on Soil]
    I --> J[Compactive Force = W_drum]
    J --> K[Compacted Granular Surface]
    L[Scraper Blade] --> I
```

**Description of Flow:**
- The engine power is routed through the gearbox to drive both front and rear drums in **tandem**.
- The ballast compartment allows variable weight by adding water or sand.
- The scraper blade continuously cleans the drum surface to prevent soil build-up.
- The contact patch on the soil receives a uniform vertical stress, ideal for **granular soil compaction**.

---

## 4.2 Pneumatic Tired Roller — Wheel Layout Diagram

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[Front Axle - Steering] --> A1[Tyre 1 - Left]
    A --> A2[Tyre 2 - Left-Center]
    A --> A3[Tyre 3 - Right-Center]
    A --> A4[Tyre 4 - Right]
    B[Rear Axle - Drive with Wobble] --> B1[Tyre 5 - Left]
    B --> B2[Tyre 6 - Left-Center]
    B --> B3[Tyre 7 - Right-Center]
    B --> B4[Tyre 8 - Right]
    C[Middle Wobble Tyre] --> C1[Tyre 9 - Offset]
    A1 --> D[Compaction via Kneading]
    A2 --> D
    A3 --> D
    A4 --> D
    B1 --> D
    B2 --> D
    B3 --> D
    B4 --> D
    C1 --> D
    D --> E[Kneading plus Sealing Action on Soil]
    E --> F[Densified Surface Layer]
```

**Description:**
- The front and rear axles carry 4 tyres each, **offset** to cover the entire width.
- The middle wobble wheel (or oscillating axle) ensures no strip is left uncompacted.
- The combined effect is a **kneading plus pressing** action that seals the surface against water.

---

## 4.3 Sheep's Foot Roller — Drum Detail

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Main Frame] --> B[Drum Assembly]
    B --> C[Drum Shell - Cylindrical]
    C --> D[Sheeps Foot Pad 1]
    C --> E[Sheeps Foot Pad 2]
    C --> F[Sheeps Foot Pad 3]
    C --> G[Sheeps Foot Pad N]
    D --> H[Small Contact Area - 30-80 cm2]
    E --> H
    F --> H
    G --> H
    H --> I[High Local Contact Pressure - 30-100 kg/cm2]
    I --> J[Penetration into Cohesive Soil]
    J --> K[Compaction of Deep Layer]
    L[Scraper Bar] --> C
    M[Ballast Tank] --> C
```

**Description:**
- The drum shell carries a regular pattern of **protruding pads**.
- Each pad has a small area, producing **very high local pressure**.
- The pads penetrate the cohesive soil, compacting **from within** rather than just at the surface.
- As the lift becomes denser, the pads **walk out** of the soil, signalling completion.

---
